title: Learning from Our Musical Roots: History of Mariachi Music
description: This course is designed to educate and inform students on the critical role of Latine music. With a specific emphasis on mariachi, we strive to expand our repertoire and historical knowledge on underrepresented music in implemented institutions. The participating students will engage with one another, allowing them to develop a sense of comunidad and familia. Can take this course for 1 or 2 units. No music experience or previous historical knowledge needed, All majors are welcome!

title: GeoWall at Berkeley
department: College of Civil and Environmental Engineering
description: The DeCal will consist of lectures, laboratories, and workshops. Lectures will teach basic and applied geotechnical principles, host industry speakers from the Geotechnical Industry, and attend field trips. Laboratory sessions will provide hands-on application of lecture materials. Workshops will range from learning how to use engineering analysis, design, and simulation softwares and provide professional development opportunities.
enrollmentInformation: We accept everyone!

title: Introduction to Technical AI Safety
description: Yoshua Bengio—the most cited living scientist and Turing Award winner—recently said in an interview that he has fully pivoted into AI Safety work because he was scared for his grandchildren’s future due to AI risk. Many reputable scholars, like Stuart Russell, Geoffrey Hinton, and Dario Amodei, ring the alarm about the catastrophic risks of AI and the need for work in AI Safety. Yet trillions of dollars are being spent on the race to build AGI, and it's accelerating by the day. There are not enough people working on AI Safety, and more technical people should work on it so humanity gets this technology right. In this Decal, you will gain an understanding of the problems in AI safety and some of the key technical research directions that aim to solve them. You will learn about the theoretical and practical risks associated with using advanced AI systems, the difficulties inherent to addressing them, and the current state of research regarding solutions.
enrollmentInformation: Admissions will be on a rolling basis. Please fill out our google form.

title: Prog Rock!
description: From the 1960s to the late 1980s, a rock n’ roll subgenre would branch throughout the United Kingdom and gain commercial success. Progressive rock, commonly known as “prog”, would push the boundaries of rock n’ roll as we know it. Combining elements of experimental music, complex compositions, and lyricism, progressive rock became an emblem of expressing social and political commentary through music. In this course, students will explore the ‘Golden Age’ of progressive rock music, listening to prog legends such as Pink Floyd, Yes, and Crimson King. Students will discuss how prog rock artists combine different musical styles, varying from jazz, classical, and instrumental music. Students will also explore subgenres of prog rock, including neo-progressive rock, progressive metal, and American progressive rock. Together, students will answer the difficult question of what makes progressive rock.
enrollmentInformation:
Enrollment is based on a first-come, first-served policy through the application below.  

You will receive an email from the facilitator once your enrollment has been confirmed. Please enroll through CalCentral with the provided course number once you have received confirmation.

title: Financial Mathematics for Actuaries
description:
This course introduces key concepts of financial mathematics and financial instruments with an emphasis on applications in the actuarial field. While it is primarily focused on the actuarial profession, the fundamentals covered in this course are also useful to students interested in finance. The curriculum is intended to prepare students for Exam 2/FM, an actuarial exam jointly sponsored and administered by the Society of Actuaries and the Casualty Actuarial Society. This course is sponsored by the California Actuarial League.
The primary goal of this course is to teach students about the conventions and practices of making financial calculations. This includes understanding how to calculate and interpret present, current, and accumulated values for various sequences of non-contingent cash flows. Particular attention will be given to annuities, loans, and bonds, as well as a discussion of portfolio management in a mathematical context. Students will also learn how varying interest rates play a role in risk analysis, behavior, and decisions regarding financial investments. The manner in which these topics are covered will be focused on relating content to Exam 2/FM, and more generally the actuarial context, but may be similarly utilized for other contexts of financial decisions. These skills provide a basis for future use in and learning regarding: reserving, valuation, pricing, asset/liability management, investment income, capital budgeting and valuing contingent cash flows.
enrollmentInformation: Students will be enrolled based on weighted lottery by application.

title: Immigration & Democracy: Who Gets to Belong?
description:
This examines how U.S. immigration law shapes democratic participation, citizenship, and political belonging. From foundational Supreme Court cases to contemporary debates, we will explore who is included and excluded within American democracy. Students will trace the historical development of immigration policy, analyze constitutional principles like due process and equal protection, and connect legal frameworks to the lived experiences of immigrants today.

The course is discussion-driven and applied. Rather than relying on a traditional textbook, students engage with curated readings, legal cases, policy briefs, and multimedia sources provided throughout the semester. Class sessions combine short lectures with interactive discussions, simulations, and collaborative activities that encourage students to think critically and speak confidently about complex legal and political issues. No prior coursework in law or ethnic studies is required, and students from all majors are welcome.

There are no traditional exams. Assessment is based on weekly discussion posts, a midterm project, and a final case analysis or policy-focused project. Attendance is mandatory, though students may miss up to three classes without penalty.
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served.

title: A Critical Understanding of Public Health
description: The purpose of this course is to engage students in active and informed discussion regarding the interdisciplinary field of Global Health. The course will explore and explain the various social, political, and cultural underpinnings of health issues. Understanding the functional tools behind delivering sustainable health care and ensuring equity in treatment involves examining global health through the frameworks of its individual disciplines. Simple terms such as “aid” and “development” need to be examined in the context of their colonial roots as well as their place in the 21st century. This course involves studying the work of current pioneers in global health as well as the various disciplines that contribute to it, including but not limited to anthropology, social medicine, sociology, health economics, and political science. Social literature and history will be taught alongside current medicine and research. Each work will be examined with the goal of ultimately equipping students with the tools to investigate current health care and development models from a holistic standpoint and encourage an understanding that will bolster their contributions to social justice in health care.
enrollmentInformation: Course code for enrollment through Cal Central: 30694

title: Copwatch: Community Based Accountability
description:
Class Overview: This class will be facilitated using lectures and guided class discussions by invited guest speakers and student facilitators. There are opportunities for direct field monitoring of police on duty. Students will learn about the issues faced by residents that racial profiling, civil, and human rights abuses bring to the community. Students will be trained in basic criminal procedure, power analysis, and techniques for observing police activity. In addition, students will examine the history and origins of police, resistance movements, and community-based alternatives to the justice system as we know it. Finally, in this course, students will have the opportunity to have a role in the community beyond the DeCal, researching topics that are current and relevant to the larger community.

Student Learning Outcomes: 
By the end of this semester, students will: 
● Know and be able to assert their rights when stopped by police. 
● Be able to identify constitutional police practices and procedures. 
● Be able to document police misconduct, preserve evidence, and evaluate a case for potential civil rights violations. 
● Be able to interact with civil authorities in pursuit of individual complaints and learn how to file Public Records Act requests. 
● Be able to discuss class topics in an in-depth manner.
● Articulate strategies for pursuing justice in cases of misconduct or unjust laws or practices. 
● Analyze the causes of police misconduct and recommend possible solutions. 

Student Requirements:
Attendance (20%) - Attendance will be taken at the beginning of each class; sign in with the course facilitators at the start of class to be marked present. Students may not have more than two unexcused absences in order to pass. Please notify facilitators via email if you need to miss class. Students should come to class having read the assigned reading and prepared to ask questions and discuss related topics in-depth. 

Copwatch Shifts (40%) - Each student is required to participate in five (5) Copwatch shifts or related activities. Shifts can include:
Copwatching (direct observation) on the street. Berkeley Copwatch will lead shifts. (At least 2 shifts must be direct observation; 1 shift may be documentation of a police encounter that did not occur on an official shift.)
Sign up for direct observation shifts here: Shift Sign Up
Class walk to Grassroots House counts as 1 general shift (non-direct observation)
Attendance at public meetings held remotely such as the Police Accountability Board, the City Council or other related public forums related to policing.
SHIFT SHEET: Make a copy of this document to fill out over the course of the semester to keep track of meetings and observations. This is due on the last day of class.

Assignments (40%) - 
GROUP PROJECT (20%): Students will identify a research question for inquiry to pursue throughout the semester. This question can be structured so that there is an opportunity to create a California Public Records Act (CPRA) request that is part of the research process. Students will be required to present research on a topic of their interest for 5 minutes and make a final presentation (many formats accepted: zines, videos, slides etc.) in partners or groups. This is due on the last day of class. Our final presentations will be held at Grassroots House. (2022 Blake St.)
REFLECTIONS (20%): Students will be required to produce four (4) reflections based on a reading, what happened in the class prior, or as a checkpoint for research while working on preparing the final project throughout the semester. These should be reflection pieces rather than a summary or direct response to a text. (1 page double spaced min., 2 max.)
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served! - legalst 198 Sec 006 #26983

title: Oral and Dental Health Decal
description:
COURSE DESCRIPTION:
The overall goal of the course is to bring awareness to the importance of proper oral hygiene
and its relation to disease. Specifically, this dental health course will discuss oral hygiene,
preventive measures, dental procedures and treatments, oral diseases, new dental technology
through interactive activities, the introduction of 5-6 specialties within dentistry, as well as the
process of applying to dental school. Note: This class is not only for Pre-Dental Students!
We welcome all students interested in Oral Hygiene.

COURSE LEARNING OBJECTIVES:
By the end of this course, students will become familiar with the basics of preventative
dentistry, oral conditions, and dental procedures, and ultimately understand how to take
care of their own teeth and oral hygiene.

METHODS OF INSTRUCTION:
The course will consist of 14 one and a half hour in-person (contingent on COVID-19 status)
class periods (lectures, discussions, quizzes, and presentations). Classes will be conducted by
course facilitator(s) and will include lectures by guest speakers from the dental field. Each
topic will have supplemental pre-lecture reading assignments provided through bCourses.

DISCUSSION/REFLECTION COMPONENT:
In this course specifically, we will discuss and reflect on the articles, websites, and readings
assigned each week. Together in class, we will explore their significance and relevance to oral
and dental health and hygiene, as well as share our personal takeaways from each week’s
materials. We encourage every student to share their thoughts and perspectives throughout the
Semester.

GETTING INTO THE COURSE:
This is the 20th semester of running this course! Because of a limited class size, students will be
admitted to the course only after completing a student survey, which the facilitators will screen
before emailing out CCNs. We hope to accept the students who are most interested in learning
about our subject. Students interested/admitted to the course are required to attend class on the
first day.

EVALUATION OF COURSE:
Students are evaluated on their attendance, quizzes (10), reflections (3), a midterm
research paper (1), and a final presentation (1). In order to receive a passing grade, a
The student must “pass” each of the following three categories:
1. Attendance: Attendance is mandatory, and lectures will be synchronous on Tuesday
6:30-8:00 pm PST. There will be 3 excused absences (must email in advance) for the
semester, and we will have an excused absence form if more absences are needed.
2. Participation (35 points): It is important for our students to be engaged
in the learning process. To encourage class participation, we ask that
students 1) ask questions and engage in class discussions AND 2) ask
questions and engage in class discussions via the bCourses Discussion
forum. For any students who need accommodations, please speak with the
Facilitators.
ASSIGNMENTS:
This category consists of 10 Quizzes, 3 Article/Lecture Reflections, and 1 Final Research Paper:

title: Rubik's Cube/Speedcubing Decal
description:
Come learn how to solve the Rubik's Cube, get faster at solving it, or master another puzzle!

Students only need to attend one section: Beginner or Advanced. The Beginner section is for anyone who doesn’t know how to solve a Rubik’s cube; if you know how to solve a cube, you should be in the Advanced section. If you’re not sure which section you belong in, ask one of the instructors at the first class or send us an email.

You will be required to have or obtain a 3x3x3 Cube. If you do not have one, we can help you find a cube. Class will be on Tues 5-6pm (Beginner), 6-7pm (Advanced).

Throughout the semester there will be some short assignments.

The course is graded on a P/NP basis, so attendance is required. Students are allowed 3 absences, excused or unexcused, throughout the entire semester.

title: ART 198 - Movement, Music, and Myth: An Introduction to Kathak
description:
This course offers an engaging, movement-focused introduction to Kathak, a classical Indian dance form originating from North India. Known for its rhythmic footwork, spins, and expressive gestures, Kathak merges storytelling with movement and music. Students will spend the majority of class time on physical instruction, with short weekly readings or videos assigned to support historical and theoretical understanding outside of class.

No prior dance experience is necessary. The course is inclusive and open to all students interested in classical arts, movement, or South Asian culture.
enrollmentInformation: We are capping this class at 15-17 students due to room capacity. It will be first come first serve / rolling admission so please sign up ASAP! Please also note that we are happy to accommodate schedule change requests so reach out if you are not available at the scheduled time.

title: Human-Centered Design
description:
Throughout the semester, students will apply what they learn while using industry standard tools to solve a problem they care about in their group midterm research report and individual final project. 

The course culminates in a case study students can use to kickstart their portfolio and greater design journey. 

The link to the application will be posted at the start of the semester.

title: Medical Microbiology
description:
Medical Microbiology DeCal: The Medical Microbiology DeCal explores the many ways that bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and viruses interact with human health. Each week, we focus on a new group of organisms and their impact on medicine — ranging from the role of yeasted breads in ancient human nutrition to how malaria shaped human genetics. Several medical specialties are introduced, including infectious disease, endocrinology, genetics, immunology, and hematology.

No prerequisites are required; the course is open to students of all backgrounds who are curious about the intersections of microbiology and medicine. The enrollment code 29010.

title: Medical Terminology
description:
Course Overview
- This course aims to provide students with a basic understanding of word parts, medical terms, abbreviations, and how they are used in relation to human body systems, disease, and treatment. Each lecture will focus on terms corresponding to the 11 human organ systems, followed by a case report review each week to practice applying those terms to a real-life example. The course will especially be helpful to students interested in a career in healthcare or a related field, but is open to students from any major at any grade level. Whether you're preparing for advanced coursework or simply seeking health literacy, this course offers valuable tools for navigating your future endeavors with confidence.
enrollmentInformation:
Anyone is welcome to join, but space is limited. 
Course Code: 29012

title: Introduction to Medical Cantonese
description:
This course aims to address a critical need for Cantonese language skills among healthcare professionals in San Francisco and the Bay Area. The lack of Cantonese language services creates substantial healthcare obstacles for Asian American residents, for whom Cantonese is the second most demanded language for bilingual assistance. This course aims to provide students with essential language skills in Cantonese to effectively communicate with Cantonese-speaking patients in a medical setting. The course will cover basic medical terminology, phrases, and communication strategies necessary for history taking, patient examination, diagnosis, and treatment discussions. Students will learn through lecture material and interactive language labs.

Students will be evaluated based on lecture attendance, quizzes, assignments, and a final project. Students must receive a cumulative grade of 70% and no more than 2 absences to pass the course. P/NP grading only.

Prerequisites: No prior knowledge of Cantonese is required. However, some knowledge of Chinese is strongly recommended.
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served.

title: A Primer on Molecular Biology
description:
This course is intended to provide an introduction to laboratory-based molecular biology. UC Berkeley is one of the finest research institutions in the world, especially in the realm of biology; therefore, science majors here have the ability to partake in ground-breaking research with world-renowned faculty. However, if accepted, there is a certain amount of information you’re expected to have learned; this information is not explicitly taught in any lower division course, and synthesizing it on your own can be daunting and difficult. 

This course is intended to bridge this gap; we will be providing an insight into the basic techniques that will be utilized in any molecular biology lab. Of course, going over every possible laboratory mechanism is impossible; for this reason, we’ll be discussing techniques that are the most common and often assigned to undergraduates when they first join a laboratory. This will facilitate your transition into the laboratory and also help you make the most of your research experience, whether you are currently working in one or intend to in the future. The instruction will be done through a mixture of lecturing and group activities during class time.
enrollmentInformation: First come, first serve. Enrollment is open to everyone until enrollment capacity is hit!

title: Language in Use
description: In this class, we will focus on the subfield of linguistics known as pragmatics, which studies how context contributes to the meaning of language. By the end of the class, we hope that students will gain a broad exposure to many different subtopics in pragmatics and a better understanding of pragmatics in their everyday lives. Throughout the course, we will draw on applied, interesting examples of pragmatics in order to make sense of the concepts of pragmatics in real-life contexts. As a linguistics class, expect to see many cross-linguistic examples as well! The class will be lecture-based, with many interspersed opportunities for discussion. Each class will cover a general topic of importance in pragmatics. Although there are no prerequisites, students will get the most out of the class if they have some familiarity with linguistics, philosophy, and/or logic. Grading will be done on a pass/no pass basis, based on attendance and a final presentation. Students will have one free absence, and two further absences can be made up by submitting one of the weekly readings with extensive annotations. Students who have perfect attendance (not including the one permitted absence) will be exempted from the final presentation.
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment Information: The enrollment code is: 22375

title: Cal Cricket Decal: Inspire and Support Aspiring Cricketers
description:
With the growing popularity of cricket in America, instilling a passion for cricket is more important than ever. This not only develops knowledgeable enthusiasts but also nurtures future cricket players and leaders in the sport. However, cultivating this enthusiasm requires a unique set of skills. This course aims to equip students with the tools, mindset, and techniques to make cricket a mainstay in our community. By teaching cricket not just as a sport, but as a holistic process of skill development and teamwork, we ensure that individuals remain enthusiastic, inquisitive, and ever-eager to enhance their cricketing abilities and knowledge.
Throughout the semester, students will delve into both the historical and cultural significance of cricket, as well as its physical aspects. They will learn how to blend engaging drills with fundamental cricketing principles, encompassing fielding, batting, and bowling.
This culminating project will be brought to life as students lead these practice sessions for a local cricket club in the Berkeley community. Designed to foster effective coaching techniques for teaching cricket in our community, this course emphasizes the importance of personalized coaching, skill development, and fostering a love for the game.
enrollmentInformation: Enroll in CHMENG 98 with CCN 21231. The course is first come, first serve and there is no other criteria for enrolling! Excited to see everyone!

title: Ambient Music
description:
Can a song be a piece of furniture? Can it cure your headache? Can it change the world? This course delves into the core tenets of ambient music as both a practice and a way of thinking. Students will read a variety of context-setting interviews and reviews, listen to several influential ambient works, and participate in various listening exercises.

After successfully completing this course, students will have studied the philosophical and musical influences behind the ambient movement, be able to apply these ideas to any musical work, and have a broader understanding of the art of sound itself. In short, students will be able to hear with a fresh perspective. The concepts of ambient music — e.g. repetition as a tool, glitch as metanarrative, etc. — are applicable to all music, and understanding them deepens our familiarity with the medium.

Classes will include both lectures and discussions of the homework (listening and reading materials). Discussion participation is a large percentage of the overall grade and will be evaluated based on how well students 1) demonstrate understanding of the material and 2) synthesize course concepts into their own ideas. Students will also submit two written assignments over the course of the semester, graded on how well they demonstrate understanding and synthesize concepts. Students who miss class will be able to make up their attendance by meeting with the instructor during office hours.
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment is still open! Class begins Tuesday, February 3rd. Use class code 24376 to enroll.

title: Music 98/198: Playing By Ear
description:
You know that song on the radio that you can’t get out of your head? Playing By Ear is a Decal centered around teaching pianists both new and experienced, on how to construct pop songs on the piano by ear and play without sheet music. For the best learning experience, it's recommended to have some experience with piano and some understanding of basic music theory (key signatures, intervals, major/minor). 

The class is formatted to have a lecture at the beginning of the class and a workshop/discussion in the latter half. There are no formal exams: your midterm and final consist of performing a song of your choice to your friends and peers!
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment is through application, so please fill out as soon as possible!

title: Living Futures: Regenerative Environmental Design Decal
description:
Green building and urbanism have seen huge advances over the last thirty years, yet the climate is reaching a point where we will all experience catastrophic and irreversible changes to the health of our planet within this decade. Considering the rate of change required to avoid the worst effects of the climate crisis and other global socio-environmental challenges, our progress can no longer be incremental. The Living Future framework, created by the International Living Future Institute, radically transforms the paradigm of design from one of doing less harm to one of actively doing good for the world. 

We will discuss bold methods for going beyond sustainability, looking at topics such as urban agriculture, net-positive design, healthy and happy neighborhoods, universal inclusion, and biophilia. With a climate justice lens, we will also examine case studies of regenerative design beyond the Global North. Within this decade, humanity must reshape its relationship with nature and realign its ecological footprint to be within the planet’s carrying capacity. This course is an act of optimism and believes that with the right wisdom and tools in the hands of passionate, sensitive individuals, a revolutionary transformation is possible.

This course is offered as Pass/No Pass. There will be no exams. Requirements for a passing grade are as follows:

– Have no more than 2 absences.

– Participate in 7 out of 11 weekly bCourses discussions in a timely manner.

– Submit the final project on time with all required elements.
enrollmentInformation: Open to all!

title: History, Culture and Practice of Salsa Dance
description:
Salsa at Cal is a group of UC Berkeley students and alumni who spread their appreciation and knowledge of Salsa and other Latin dances to students on campus. We host dance events, trips to local clubs, and FREE classes in Salsa and other styles. We've taught everything from Rueda Cubana to Bachata, but our main focus is on Salsa.

This DeCal, taught by members of our Latin Fusion Performance Team, is an opportunity for UC Berkeley students to learn salsa and bachata with NO PRIOR EXPERIENCE REQUIRED. We have two levels of skill, beginner and intermediate, that we offer on Mondays starting February 2nd.

The link to apply to the DeCal can be found on our Instagram (attached below as the course website). Can't wait to see you in class!

title: Economic Tools for Effective Altruism
description:
Effective altruism (EA) is a social movement to find the best ways to do good, with our lim-
ited time, money, and resources. This course also serves to inform students how they can “do
good” in as effective a way as possible with their careers, especially within economics and re-
lated fields (including, but not limited to CS, applied math, epidemiology, political science, and
neuroscience).

We combine practical lessons from economics with theoretical concepts from the philosophy of
ethics to equip students with introductory skills that can help them identify and work toward
solving some of the world’s most pressing problems.

People involved in effective altruism tend to agree that, partly due to uncertainty about which
cause is best, we should split our resources between problems. But they don’t agree on what that
split should be. Applying the economic concepts of efficiency to the domain of morality, effective
altruists aim not just to make the world better, but to make it as good as possible.

Finally, we give you some time at the end of the program to begin to reflect on how you person-
ally can help to solve these problems. We don’t expect you’ll have an answer by the end of the
course, but we hope you’re better prepared to explore this further.

In-person attention is expected, but feel free to email the instructors beforehand if extenuating circumstances arise, we err on the side of being accommodating. Weekly homework will consist of online readings and a bCourses quiz (open-ended questions graded on completion) to keep students on track for the in-person discussion. Students are expected to read the weekly content and to take the "quiz" before class. The last two weeks of class will consist of student presentations. Students will form groups to present on a sub-topic of their choosing within the broader topics of Effective Altruism (Global Development, AI Safety, Animal Welfare, Biosecurity).

Please fill out the google form at: https://forms.gle/FqWFR3492fGyzhJMA 

Make sure to check the box that says you are interested in taking the DeCal/Intro Fellowship and fill out the short response. We will follow up with a 5 minute Zoom/Google Meet chat to get you enrolled afterwards! Thank you so much!

title: Stem Cells: Topics in Regenerative Biology
description:
Many believe that the cures to most diseases lie in stem cell science and research, yet others fear repercussions that could forever alter our social conscience and morality. This class takes students on a journey inside the stem cell revolution, where scientists, researchers, policy-makers, and ethicists will challenge students to consider the societal, political, even spiritual implications of this rapidly developing frontier.

The Stem Cell Decal aims to creatively provide students with a basic understanding of stem cells and stem cell research. We cover four modules over twelve weeks: Science and Biology, Engineering and Technology, Ethics and Controversy, and Policy and Advocacy.
These topics will be explored through weekly one hour lectures on a variety of stem cell topics, featuring both guest speakers and facilitators. Hour-long discussion sections led by facilitators will be held weekly to revisit new material introduced in lecture, present pressing stem cell issues in recent scientific literature and news media, and urge conversation among students about current developments in the field.

After taking this course, students will have a basic understanding of stem cell science, its potential and applications, the controversy behind it, and its role in society. We hope students will possess enough knowledge to understand and participate in stem cell activities on campus and beyond, whil also formulating their own position on the political stances surrounding stem cell research.

Lecture-Monday-6-7PM
Discussion-Wednesday-6-7PM 
MCELLBI 98 Section 002 (class #: 23662)

title: Introduction to Full Stack Development
description:
This is Introduction to Full Stack Web Development, presented by Web Development at Berkeley.

In this course we cover the basics of web development. This includes everything from the fundamental frameworks that ground web development, the inner workings of the Internet itself, and many of the frameworks, technologies, and best practices that are used in the industry today.

No prior CS experience is required to participate and succeed in this course, but we do acknowledge that we cover a fairly large amount of material and that makes this course fairly heavy, even for those with prior CS experience.

In the spirit of full-stack development, we cover the three main pillars of modern web development: backend development, frontend development, and design.

Every week you will attend 2 lectures, each 1.5 hours. Most lectures will have a short vitamin assignment accompanying them and you will typically have a project due every 3 weeks to a month. Course staff will be holding office hours regularly throughout the week to provide guidance and feedback on the assignments. Towards the end of the semester your work in this class will culminate in a final project with a project group.
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment is by application. Complete and submit the Google Form below. We will provide accepted students with a unique enrollment code after applications have been reviewed.

title: Side Hustles for Skill-Building and Extra Income
description:
This course is designed to empower students with the knowledge, skills, and mindset needed to launch and manage successful side hustles while balancing their academic commitments. Meeting once a week, the course explores various aspects of side hustles, from idea generation to execution, with a strong emphasis on practical applications. It highlights the value of building monetizable skills and the importance of diversifying income streams. Students will have the opportunity to develop their own side hustle proposals, initiate their ventures, reflect on their overall entrepreneurial journeys and have the chance to participate in a SharkTank-style event with real monetary investments and esteemed judges (sharks!).

By the end of this course, students will be able to:
. Identify viable side hustle opportunities aligned with their skills and interests
. Conduct basic market research to validate side hustle ideas
. Develop a business plan for a side hustle
. Understand the financial considerations of starting a side business
. Implement effective marketing strategies for their side hustle
. Manage time effectively to balance academic commitments and entrepreneurial pursuits
. Network and build relationships to support the growth of their side hustle
. Launch and manage a side hustle, adapting to challenges as they arise

Throughout the course, students will engage in weekly assignments designed to deepen their understanding of side hustles and encourage practical application of course concepts. These assignments will include a variety of tasks, such as Al-based reflections, creative problem-solving exercises, and strategic planning activities. To maintain engagement and accountability, students will also submit weekly 1-minute video check-ins, sharing updates on their progress, challenges, and insights. At the end of the course, students will create a longer video presentation that reflects on their journey of starting and managing their side hustle, highlighting key milestones, challenges, and lessons learned. Additionally, students will write two reflection papers-one at midterm and one at the end of the course-that provide a deeper
analysis of their entrepreneurial experiences, personal growth, and the skills they have developed. This structure ensures that students can actively apply what they learn, while also engaging in meaningful self-reflection to solidify their insights. Students' active participation and regular attendance are vital for their success in the course. If a student needs to miss a class, they are expected to notify the instructor in advance and take responsibility for catching up on missed material. This course is designed to provide students with a supportive, engaging, and practical introduction to the world of side hustles. The primary
goal is not just for students to start a side hustle, but to help them develop valuable skills and gain experiences that will serve them throughout their academic and professional lives. It offers an exciting opportunity for students to embark on an entrepreneurial journey filled with growth and learning.
enrollmentInformation: Please fill out the application below and enrollment information will be given accordingly.

title: Berkeley Scientific Journal: Science Journalism
description:
Berkeley Scientific is the premier undergraduate research journal of the University of  California, Berkeley. Every semester, our undergraduate staff publishes independent research done by undergraduates at UC Berkeley, interviews with faculty members, and articles on current issues in science.

The focus of the journal is broad, spanning scientific disciplines ranging from ecology to engineering, from astronomy to biochemistry. Our intended audience  includes, but is not limited to, UC Berkeley students, faculty, and the surrounding community.

The purpose of the DeCal is to give journal members a chance to learn more about the  history, production, and career opportunities in scientific journalism, while opening up work on  the journal to a wider group of individuals. BSJ members in recent semesters have included students from various disciplines including MCB, physics, economics, public health, business  administration, and many others.  

The course is designed to teach  students the fundamentals of science writing, including such basic skills as critically reading a  professional journal article, and thinking about the scientific method. This course falls within  the scope of the MCB academic program, but is open to students outside the major and will be  instructive to students in all branches of the sciences as science writing is critical to all scientific  disciplines.
enrollmentInformation:
Prospective members must apply at the link below; we have a limited capacity so will be unable to admit everyone, but base our decisions off of demonstrated interest, curiosity, and writing abilities. There are no requirements regarding major, journalism experience, or year in undergraduate career and we encourage everyone with an interest to apply. 

Those placed in a writing department will also have the option to double as an intern for our design, publicity & finance, and/or copy departments. However this year we also have a few openings for those who want to serve only in the design department; that application will be linked at the bottom of the general application (you need not apply to be a writer in order to complete the design application).

title: Introduction to Prevention in Internal Medicine
description: This course serves as an important introduction to both medical reasoning and practice. It introduces concepts of public health for pre-healthcare majors, highlighting the role of the individual healthcare practitioner in preventing common causes of morbidity and mortality. Subspecialties within internal medicine and their related preventive medicine strategies will be explored. Through educating on the intersectionality of two medical specialties - preventative medicine and internal medicine- students will be given the resources to better support the healthcare needs of the community. In addition, as a result of this course, students will become cognizant of the social determinants and lifestyle influences affecting medicine.
enrollmentInformation: Please fill out the application form and we will send you a code to enroll in the course!

title: Introduction to Psychedelic Science
description:
Introduction to Psychedelic Science, affectionately known as PsySci, will give students a survey knowledge of the growing field of psychedelic study. 

Throughout the semester, a dynamic roster of well-respected figures will join us on Thursday evenings to discuss their psychedelic niche. You will come away from PsySci with competence and continued curiosity in the psychedelic areas of neurobiology, the therapeutic paradigm, indigenous perspectives and relationality, ethics of access, policy, harm reduction, philosophy of mind, and mycology, to name a few.

PsySci is suitable for anyone, including the psychonaut fledgling and expert of altered states, and anyone outside and in between. All we ask is for your desire to learn and uphold the community values of mindfulness, openness, and kindness. Because of that, please only apply if you can commit to attending class every week. This DeCal is in high demand and the classroom is unfortunately only so big.
As well, field trips to labs, gardens, and other locales of psychedelic prominence will support our learning.
Mush love

title: Journalism 101: Taught by The Daily Californian
description:
Journalism is a backbone of American democracy, and provides an important check in democratic processes that affect the broader public. This DeCal course is designed to equip students who are not familiar with journalism with the experience and materials to apply to The Daily Californian or any other journalistic organizations by the end of the semester. During the course, students will learn how to properly structure articles from different departments (ex. Arts & Entertainment, News, Opinion, Sports). Students will write in various styles expected of creative journalists, arts reporters and sports reporters.                        

This DeCal course is designed for students without any journalism experience. Students from all majors and of all backgrounds are welcome to apply! We will meet weekly on Mondays from 6:30-8pm. 

Applications for the spring 2026 semester are due Jan. 26 at 11:59 p.m. and decisions will be released Jan 31. Students who are accepted must attend the first course to keep their spot, which will be on Monday, 2/2. APPLY HERE: tinyurl.com/dcdecals26

Note: THIS IS NOT AN APPLICATION TO THE DAILY CALIFORNIAN.
enrollmentInformation: Please apply in the google form linked!

title: Medical Racism
description:
Interested in learning more about disparities within our healthcare system? Sign up for our Medical Racism DeCal! We’ll focus on the structural institutions that create and maintain systems of oppression in healthcare, as well as the consequences of medical racism for patients. We'll begin with an array of historical examples ranging from government experiments to stereotypes about pain perception, and follow these ideas to see how they manifest in both today's medical education and modern ideas like "race-based" genetic medicine. At the same time, we'll dive into the social, economic, and political factors that create health disparities outside of the clinic.
Our weekly meetings are from 6:00 - 8:00 PM on Tuesdays with a mix of group discussions, class readings, and guest speakers. To find our course on CalCentral, just enter our course code (22646) in the enrollment search bar. Class starts February 3rd in Dwinelle 246—hope to see you there!
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served! To find our course on CalCentral, just enter our course code (22646) in the enrollment search bar.

title: The Art and Science of the Human Voice
description:
Have you ever wanted to learn how to do a “different” voice? Or wondered how human voices are actually produced?

In The Art and Science of the Human Voice, students explore the voice from multiple perspectives—biological, artistic, and social—through hands-on practice and interdisciplinary discussion. Topics include vocal anatomy and acoustics, gender and voice modification, voice acting and singing, phonetics, sociolinguistics, and voice technologies.

Coursework ranges from guided voice exercises and ear training to written reflections and peer feedback. This is a fun, 1-unit course with no prior experience required—just curiosity, a willingness to experiment, and an openness to making some strange noises in class. Expect a fun, low-pressure environment where you’ll learn how your voice works, and gain tools to analyze and intentionally shape your own voice.
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served

title: The Future of Healthcare
description: This class connects students with different healthcare professionals each week. From MDs to hospital administrators, user experience designers, tech company VPs, A.I. developers, and many more industry thought leaders. Get potential internships and research experience. Create a powerful professional network in a few weeks that would otherwise take years to develop.
enrollmentInformation: First come, First serve

title: Magic: the Gathering™: the Class
description: This class is designed to teach people to play the first successful trading card game, Magic: the Gathering.  This class is designed for new players, but current players are welcome to join.  Class topics include learn to play, card design, deckbuilding, etc.  All students will be given welcome decks that will be used throughout the class. These will be supplemented with bulk cards.  This class is 1 or 2 units.
enrollmentInformation: Enrolment priority is given to people who do not know how to play.  To get an enrolment code, you need to fill out the form with your Berkeley email.

title: Introduction to Origami Art and Design
description:
This course is for students interested in picking up a new hobby, and for students already experienced with origami looking to broaden and deepen their skills. No prior experience with origami is required! 

The first half of the semester is dedicated to learning the basics of folding origami, along with a bit of history. The second half we dive into the underlying principles of origami design. By the end of the semester, students will be able to fold complex models and design simple models of their own.
enrollmentInformation: Please look out for an email about joining the class, I hope to see you there!

title: Communicating Nature Through Art
description: This student-facilitated DeCal explores how art can be a powerful tool for environmental awareness, advocacy, and social change. Through various creative mediums—storytelling, music, film, photography, painting, and more—students will engage with nature and express their perspectives on environmental issues. The course will feature guest speakers from different artistic and environmental fields who will share insights on using art to inspire action and communicate ecological themes. Additionally, students will have the opportunity for field site visits at local natural spaces (such as the UC Botanical Garden, Tilden Park, and nearby UC Natural Reserves & Field Stations) to gather inspiration for their work. The course will be structured around a semester-long creative project, with regular check-ins, an initial proposal and outline, a research phase, a rough draft, peer feedback, and a final presentation. Students unable to attend field trips will have to do an alternative trip and propose their own outdoor experience. By the end of the course, participants will have created a piece that reflects their artistic interpretation of environmental awareness, fostering both personal creativity and collective discussion on the intersection of nature and art.

title: Scientific Responsibility & Citizenship
description:
This is an adaptation of CHEM 299, a mandatory course for first year graduate students that was recently added. In this course, graduate students were introduced to different case studies addressing ethical questions raised by modern scientific studies along with their social and environmental impacts. The course guided the students through a healthy discussion of the topics and encouraged them to apply the things they learned to their own research practices. We wanted to bring this to undergraduates as well so they see that there is a community at Berkeley that cares about the ethical and environmental impacts of the cutting edge research that is being done every day.

By the end of this course, students will develop a comprehensive understanding of scientific responsibility and citizenship, recognizing the intricate interplay between scientific advancement and societal impacts. We will explore the historical and contemporary dimensions of scientific research, critically examining how social structures and inequalities shape scientific practices. Though engaging with diverse perspectives and social theories, students will cultivate the skills to navigate ethical dilemmas, advocate for inclusive research practices, and contribute meaningfully to the advancement of science for the betterment of society.

Link to Syllabus: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1YS6lus9mc_kMwksD3APhOu10OKmX0BwH4bN6byMQCho/edit?usp=sharing

Some Highlighted Classes you might find interesting : 
- Mercury Contents of Bay Area Waters
- Berkeley & Nuclear Research
- The Racial History of the Spirometer
- Hormonal Birth Control
enrollmentInformation: GRP 004 #21204 / Regular Academic Session / 1.00 units / First Day is Feb 2nd

title: Understanding Healthcare
description:
Every person in the class, in one way or another, has interacted with the healthcare system. With the growing inequality in America and the large proportion of uninsured Americans, chances are that everyone knows someone that has been adversely affected by not having health insurance or by having a health insurance plan that is overly burdensome. 

Much of what this class is designed to do is to provide a broad perspective of the most pressing current issues in healthcare. Whether you are a prospective politician, doctor, public health professional, economist, or simply someone who will utilize healthcare in the future, this class will teach the fundamentals of healthcare policy in the United States and around the world and give you the tools to navigate the healthcare system yourself. The class will also help you make more informed decisions when voting and effectively debate issues regarding healthcare policy. This will be accomplished through current event discussions, activities, and debates about the topics.

title: Young Minds Matter! Mental Health in Adolescence
description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of child and adolescent mental health through the lens of healthcare systems, education, policy, and societal influences. Students will examine the biological, psychological, and social factors that shape mental well-being, as well as the roles of families, schools, and communities in supporting young people. Topics include developments and interventions, disparities in access to care, the impact of trauma and social determinants of health, and the intersection of mental health within our societal systems. By gaining an understanding of current research and insights from clinical expertise, case studies, and policy initiatives, students will develop a broader, holistic perspective on the challenges and opportunities in addressing youth mental health at both an individual and systemic level.

title: 21st Century Stoic: Wielding Stoicism in an Age of Dystopia
description:
This course will examine concepts of dystopia through literature, and will consider how we might utilize Stoic thought in coming to terms with these futures. In the digital age, we are constantly kept aware of global disasters, which forces us to confront these dystopian futures daily. We will discuss how we can wield the tools of philosophy in understanding and accepting these realities. The aim of this course is to redirect feelings of helplessness in the face of these catastrophes to weigh, through Stoicism, what is and is not in our control as individuals. We will contrast dystopian themes with our lived reality, and discuss what we as individuals can do to mitigate these outcomes. 

Attendance for all sessions is mandatory, as the class will be largely discussion based. Think of this as a stoic/ dystopian book club, which aims to foster your own critical thinking rather than us lecturing at you for hours on end.

The course will include two assessments, the first of which will be a presentation on a piece of dystopian literature or film (outside of the course materials) to analyze in a 7-minute presentation through the lens of stoicism. The second assessment will be a final paper contrasting the literary and philosophical themes we’ve been exploring with a current event or piece of media that especially speaks to the current world order, and to analyze it through the lens of Stoicism.
enrollmentInformation: Please fill out the Google form so we can gauge interest in the course and send out enrollment codes! (If you filled out the form before 01/21, we would appreciate if you could resubmit it, as the previous version wasn't taking down emails).

title: Decolonizing Environmentalism
description:
Decolonizing Environmentalism is an introductory course that centers global environmental knowledge and the histories and struggles of Indigenous peoples against colonial exploitation. The majority of mainstream environmental education is rooted in Western frameworks and overlooks the vast network of Indigenous stories that are crucial to understanding how we can restore our ecosystems. Through archival research and in-person consultation with environmentalists and activists from these communities, the course explores the lasting impacts of colonialism on peoples and ecosystems across Africa, Asia, Oceania, and the Americas. Students will engage with decolonial theory and climate education to understand more equitable approaches to environmental solutions. The course ultimately aims to lay the groundwork for future, deeper exploration of decolonial perspectives in environmentalism. 

For questions, please send an email to decolonizingenvironmentalism@gmail.com!

title: UC Hogwarts: The Wonderful Wizarding World of Harry Potter
description:
The objective of this course is to explore J.K. Rowling’s world in the Harry Potter series through book discussions and activities. In this course, we will help legitimize the genre of fantasy by exploring topics, themes, and issues brought about by this series. In addition, we will try to understand what makes the series so compelling to generations of readers and what has made it the best-selling book series of all time. We hope to prove that Harry Potter is a work of academic merit to our students. 
Students should be able to not just read the whole series and understand the plot, but also critically think about Harry Potter’s relevance to our modern world. We expect students to analyze the characters’ motivations, plot developments, and J.K. Rowling’s influence until today. We really have one question, for which we hope everyone can generate a different answer: J.K. Rowling’s Harry Potter is a timeless tale — why?

Each class will usually be split into two parts. The first part is a fun activity based on that week's reading, for which you can win points for your house toward the illustrious House Cup. The second part is a discussion, a reading quiz, and a short lecture concerning that week's reading. House points will also be awarded for various things from making Harry Potter-themed food to house bonding and submitting your own discussion questions.

Attendance is mandatory. If you have an excused absence, we still expect you to review the lecture slides and complete the weekly quiz. We are lenient and here to support students, and as such only ask that they communicate their needs to us. There is mandatory two-page, double-spaced Written Response to the series and the secondary readings. In lieu of a final, you will have a final project. You must submit a project proposal to the instructors by the first week of May. The final project is your own creative interpretation of the material covered throughout the course. It can be whatever you want: a story written in the world of Harry Potter, an illustration of some sort, a video, a game, or performance art —anything you like.

title: A Primer on Molecular Biology
description:
This course is intended to provide an introduction to laboratory-based molecular biology. UC Berkeley is one of the finest research institutions in the world, especially in the realm of biology; therefore, science majors here have the ability to partake in ground-breaking research with world-renowned faculty. However, if accepted, there is a certain amount of information you’re expected to have learned; this information is not explicitly taught in any lower division course, and synthesizing it on your own can be daunting and difficult. 

This course is intended to bridge this gap; we will be providing an insight into the basic techniques that will be utilized in any molecular biology lab. Of course, going over every possible laboratory mechanism is impossible; for this reason, we’ll be discussing techniques that are the most common and often assigned to undergraduates when they first join a laboratory. This will facilitate your transition into the laboratory and also help you make the most of your research experience, whether you are currently working in one or intend to in the future. The instruction will be done through a mixture of lecturing and group activities during class time.
enrollmentInformation: First come, first serve. Everyone is welcome to join the class until enrollment capacity is hit!

title: Rubik's Cube / Speedcubing Decal
description:
Come learn how to solve the Rubik's Cube, get faster at solving it, or master another puzzle!

Students only need to attend one section: Beginner or Advanced. The Beginner section is for anyone who doesn’t know how to solve a Rubik’s cube; if you know how to solve a cube, you should be in the Advanced section. If you’re not sure which section you belong in, ask one of the instructors at the first class or send us an email.

You will be required to have or obtain a 3x3x3 Cube. If you do not have one, we can help you find a cube. Class will be on Tues 5-6pm (Beginner), 6-7pm (Advanced).

Throughout the semester there will be some short assignments.

The course is graded on a P/NP basis, so attendance is required. Students are allowed 3 absences, excused or unexcused, throughout the entire semester.

title: Free Speech at Berkeley
description: A 32-hour sit-in. Required loyalty oaths for faculty. Bloody Thursday. The legacy of free speech at  Berkeley is far from just a handful of protests from the 1960s, but rather a myriad of complex movements and influential individuals that paved the way for political and personal expression. Sponsored by the Berkeley Forum, this course will educate and engage students in UC Berkeley’s historical relationship with free speech and civil disobedience, from the 1964 Free Speech Movement to the 2022 UC-UAW Strike. Students from all majors are welcome to join us for a discussion-based look at the past, present, and future of campus discourse. Please email us with any questions!

title: SwingCal
description:
SwingCal is a one unit, pass no pass, dance elective course. We teach the basics of the Lindy Hop, a partnered swing dance that originated in Harlem, New York in the late 1920s. Class time is split between short lectures on swing culture and history, and practical lessons. We start with the basic six-count steps, progress to the Charleston, and work our way up to more layered eight-count moves and line dances. Lessons will be taught by Cal students, alumni, and guest instructors from around the bay! 

If you have questions about the course, or want to learn more about the local Lindy Hop scene, please email swingcal@gmail.com.
enrollmentInformation: Students must attend the decal infosession on Monday, January 26th from 7-9pm in order to receive a course enrollment code.

title: Bad Artists: The Ethics of Supporting Your Idol
description:
It is all too often that we find ourselves markedly starstruck by an artist. Be it by their prose, musical composition, act, or illustration, exposure to such art can be transformative; enlightening; illuminating; so much so that many (and we ourselves) cannot help but place them upon pedestals of pure light. But in the internet age, it’s near impossible to not know of their wrongs—where do we draw the line? Why, and when, do we allow the wool to be pulled over our eyes? 

This course is an exploration of the morals and ethics in the consumption of work by problematic artists through a spectrum of artistic mediums. There is a question that has plagued the human condition from its fateful inception: what the hell is up with Kanye West? The goal of this class is to aid students in establishing their own personal system of parameters that they can then utilize to examine their responses to the art they read, watch, listen and look at by the perpetrators of societal conduct.  

Together, we will investigate artists from the pantheon of violation (racism, sexism, hypocrisy, etc.) and attempt to decipher on a case-by-case basis the psychic degradation that occurs when we violate our principles through consumption. Creatives include, but are not limited to, Ronald Dahl, Virginia Woolf, Woody Allen, The Smiths, and others.
enrollmentInformation: Application

title: Crude Awakening: Oil & Gas Fundamentals
description:
Oil and gas remain central to the global energy system—even as societies work urgently toward decarbonization. This course examines the tension between reliance on petroleum and the global push for transition: despite ambitious climate goals and rapid growth in renewables, oil and gas still provide over 50% of the world’s energy. From plastics and fertilizers to transportation and trade, petroleum products remain deeply embedded in modern life.

This student-led course takes an interdisciplinary approach to understanding the oil and gas industry. Students will explore how resources are formed, produced, refined, and traded; how they influence economies, geopolitics, and security; and how they intersect with climate change, environmental justice, and sustainability. Alongside global case studies, the course highlights California’s distinctive role in oil production, policy, and climate debates, offering both local and international perspectives on energy, environment, and transition.

By combining technical foundations with economic, political, and ethical analysis, the course equips students with tools to critically evaluate how the oil and gas industry operates today and how it may evolve in a world working to reduce dependence on fossil fuels.
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served. No application required. Course number 30110

title: Young Adult Literature
description: The objective of this course is to analyze literary devices used within “Young Adult” literature and the manner in which they facilitate discussion within their intended audience about the socio-political and economic environment of their creation. We will be exploring the rise and fall of specific trends, archetypes and themes unique to this genre, and recognize the psychological motivations behind characters that make them appealing to the target audience. The course will also investigate the publishing industry behind the books and the process to publication. Ultimately, students should gain an appreciation for the great nuance within the genre and an in-depth understanding of the literary value that the “Young Adult” genre offers.
enrollmentInformation: We will accept applications on a rolling basis in waves!

title: Directed Group Studies for Underrepresented Undergraduates
description:
As a means to promote academic excellence, EE 98 is a DeCal for underrepresented undergraduate students at UC Berkeley. Since the course is hosted and maintained by the Black Engineering and Science Student Association, our limited resources are used to prioritize BESSA members, but membership is not required. This class will provide students with direct access to tutors and resources to raise their grade point average and offer 1 unit of academic credit. The course is led by BESSA’s STEM Excellence Chair and there are no projects, exams, or quizzes. The only required assignments are a weekly check-in form, another feedback form that will be released towards the end of the semester, and weekly attendance on Monday from 6:00 - 8:00 PM. 

Classes Supported: EECS 16A, 16B; Math 1A/16A, 1B/16B, 53, 54, 55; Physics 7A, 7B, 8A, 8B; Data C8; Chemistry 1A, 3A, 3B; Computer Science 61A, 61B, 61C, 70; Biology 1A, 1B, E10; Mechanical Engineering 102B, 106; Stats 20; English R1A/B …more

title: Criminal Psychology
description:
Course Description: 
The course aims to analyze the mind of criminals, particularly of those who commit heinous crimes, which include murder, sexual assault/rape, and arson. We begin with the evolution of crime and seek to “explain” what factors influence these criminals, such as environment, mental illnesses, and personality. We will examine real cases of past criminals to learn what shaped them. We will learn what discoveries researchers found, and how these discoveries are used to create profiles. And to top it all off, we will see how law enforcement has handled these cases, and the efficacy of forensic psychologists when used in law and order (i.e. how police and courts use forensic psychologists). 
At the end of the course, students are expected to understand: 
• What may “create” or shape a criminal to commit heinous crimes 
• How real past criminals behaved, with examples 
• Criminal profiles and their accuracy 
• The efficacy of forensic psychologists 
• How law enforcement and the legal system use forensic psychologists
enrollmentInformation: We use an application system, and our main focus when reviewing applications is student interest! Fill out the Google Form below and we can't wait to read it!

title: Adulting
description: We are excited to announce that the Adulting Decal is open for Spring 2026! The Adulting Decal teaches you necessary life skills, including how to develop good habits, manage stress, pay taxes, budget your spending, and live a healthy lifestyle. Given that the school system does not teach students how to live in the real world and function as an adult, this course explores the many dimensions of how to successfully become an adult. If you feel unprepared in terms of your general “adulting” skills, our Decal is here to help! Our class will be held on Tuesdays from 5-6:30pm.
enrollmentInformation: In order to gain access to our course, you must apply! We will link the application here. The deadline to submit your application is Friday, January 30th, 2026 at 11:59pm.

title: ASDecal: Understanding Autism
description:
ASDeCal: Understanding Autism is an introductory course to the basics and nuances of living with Autism Spectrum Disorder. As biology and cognition do not hold all the answers, and we will also go over the sociopolitical side of Autism, such as healthcare inequalities, workplace discrimination, and how intersectional experiences such as prejudice or discrimination, differences in culture, support needs/visibility of disability, SES, etc. may impact and shape an individual on the spectrum's experiences. There will also be discussions that analyze how larger systemic institutions (such as the American healthcare industrial complex, the police state, capitalism, etc.) affect those on the spectrum, as well as discussing changes that could be made to or how Autistic people can learn to better navigate them. Students will also hear from guest speakers involved in the many different fields of Autism research/support, with the majority of our speakers being neurodivergent themselves.

As a whole, this class looks at Autism with a focus on intersectionality and helps students to not only better understand the lived experiences of Autistic individuals, but also think critically about how we categorize, label, and interact with people on all parts of the Autism spectrum.
enrollmentInformation: First come first serve! Fill out our google form so we can send send more info about course enrollment!

title: Cal History, Spirit, and Traditions
description: This course will dive into the rich history, remarkable spirit, and spectacular traditions of Cal from its founding in 1868 to the present-day. We will touch on the origins of the Stanford Axe, the Free Speech Movement, and even some current events at the University. Our hope is that this course will allow you to love and appreciate Cal even more. The majority of our lectures will feature an expert on the subject at hand, including professors, alumni, and friends of the University. The course will include a paper, a midterm, and a final project. From “The Play” to the Cal Marching Band, we’ll explore it all in the Cal History, Spirit, and Traditions DeCal!

title: Hidden Intersections: Addressing Systemic Bias in Stem
description: What does a scientist look like, or act like? What kinds of questions are “scientific”? Most of us have been taught, consciously or unconsciously, a set of answers to these questions. In this decal, we want to open up discussion around the traditional narrative of science, and explore the stories that often get overlooked. The first half of the course will be concerned mainly with study the narrative of science; how the story of science has been told, who is telling those stories, and how this privileges some people and leaves out others. In the second half of the course, we will ask what it means to be an ethical scientist and explore how society and science intersect. Throughout, there will be an emphasis on students actively engaging with the ideas of the course in their own lives; the two mini-projects and a final capstone are the main examples of this.

title: Introduction to Kidney Disease
description: Introduction to Kidney Disease is a 2 unit (P/NP) DeCal that will elucidate various aspects of kidney disease including its types, general pathogenesis, health disparities, therapies, and pertinent careers. Allowing students to apply concepts from their core classes, this DeCal will synthesize various topics such as public health, immunology, and anatomy to the context of Kidney Disease. It will also help students understand and navigate through scientific research. Students will be evaluated on their understanding of course material via 2 exams, an essay, a seminar, and assignments. This class is primarily geared towards students interested in human disease (e.g. pre-health, pre-PhD, pre-biotech, etc.), but students of all interests are welcome to join. No prior knowledge in biology or medicine is required. We only ask for your interest and curiosity in kidney disease!

title: The NewSpace Economy & Entrepreneurship Course
description:
Structured like a fellowship, this two-semester course educates students of all backgrounds about Space 2.0 and gives them the experience of working on a project with a space company. In the lecture segment of the course, students will learn about the NewSpace Economy in the first semester and entrepreneurship in the second from the course instructor and industry guests. In the project segment of the course, students will be fellows for a space technology startup and will work on technical and business projects while receiving guidance and feedback from company mentors. We welcome all majors in this course!

The class is composed of both a lecture and company project component per week. For the lecture component, students will be expected to attend in-person synchronous lectures every week. For the project component, students will be expected to spend at least 5 hours per week – at least 1 hour with their project team weekly and/or with their company mentors and then an additional 4 hours minimum per week on their own for the company projects.

title: Wargaming
description:
Wargaming is the use of games to model social conflicts.  Wargaming has been historically been used as a tool to assist planners for combat scenarios.  In modern times, more applications are being made to test and explore other areas of social science including negotiations and resource application.  

In this class, we will learn about and critique games and their applicability as wargames, hear from leading researchers and experts in the field of wargaming, and have 2 projects to make wargames in groups.
