Course Start Date: Monday, February 2nd
Course End Date: Wednesday, April 29th
Monday: Lab @ Studio 10C Jacobs Hall | 7:30pm - 8:30pm
Wednesday: Lecture @ 1165 Etcheverry Hall | 6:30pm - 7:30pm  
Course Value: 2 units
Expected Course Workload: Attendance required with 6–8 hours per week outside of class

According to the World Health Organization, “only 1 in 10 people in need has access to assistive products, including prostheses and orthoses, because of their high cost and because of lack of awareness, availability, trained personnel”. There are simply not enough healthcare professionals with the time and resources to create all the needed personalized assistive devices, leading to a disparity between patients that could be helped by personalized healthcare and patients that will receive personalized healthcare. The lack of assistive devices extends heavily to the animal world: throughout the past months we had to work exceptionally hard to find shelter animals that would benefit from prosthetics. This is because animals with disabilities are often put down due to reduced adoptability, without an assistive device for mobility around these animals are very hard to care for.

Through the 3D-printing for Prosthetics DeCal, we plan to address these obstacles of high cost, lack of awareness, and most importantly, trained personnel. The course will cover the fundamentals of additive manufacturing, design considerations for prosthetics, and include a hands-on project for student teams to make prosthetics shelter animals in the Bay Area.

Animal prosthetic technology is well behind human prosthetic technology. As a result, students will have the opportunity to do a literature review of human prosthetic technology and apply these new advancements in their project when designing prosthetics for animals.

Source:https://iris.who.int/server/api/core/bitstreams/0288426b-1f89-4fc8-ab83-a653107a657e/content
enrollmentInformation:
The 3D-printing for Prosthetics DeCal is open to all majors

This DeCal is intentionally interdisciplinary. Based on guidance from the Director of Operations at 3DPets, we admit students in three groups to ensure each team has a strong mix of skills:
10 Engineering/Design seats - CAD, prototyping, 3D printing, FEA simulation, iteration

10 Bio/Premed/Health seats - literature review, anatomy/biomechanics, tissue safety, comfort/fit considerations, translating veterinary needs into design requirements

10 Open seats - any student excited to learn new tools with strong communication, outreach, and project management skills
After enrollment, students are split into teams of ~6. Each team is assigned one shelter animal to make a prosthetic for through a guided design-and-build process with input from prosthetists, engineers, and veterinary professionals.

title: Contemplating Commercialization: An Introduction to Drug Discovery and Development
description: Contemplating Commercialization is an 11-week long introductory course designed for students who are interested in drug discovery and development and want to learn how they can join the industry. The first few weeks of the course are lecture-based (in-person) and will delve into each point of the  process of drug development. The second half of the course will be focused on group work, in which you will be asked to present a business plan for a fictional drug (well, fictional during the period of the course). Overall, this course will provide students with an engaging and comprehensive look into the drug development process and give them valuable experience that they can utilize when creating their own biotech company or contributing to another. Upon completion of the course, students will have an ample understanding of the opportunities available for them and what it takes to access them. There are no readings for this course.
enrollmentInformation: Student will be sent enrollment permission codes after we review all applicants.

title: UG Scientific Responsibility & Citizenship
description:
This course is an undergraduate adaptation of CHEM 299, a required course for all first year Chemistry graduate students that was recently implemented. Through this course, students were introduced to different case studies addressing ethical questions raised by modern scientific studies along with their social and environmental impacts. The course guided the students through a healthy discussion of the topics and encouraged them to apply what they learned into their own research practices. Students were also expected to participate in healthy discussion with their peers to broaden their perspectives and consider the impacts their own research had outside of the scientific world. We wanted to bring this to undergraduates as well so they see that there is a community at Berkeley that cares about the ethical and environmental impacts of the cutting edge research that is being done every day. 

By the end of this course, students will develop a comprehensive understanding of scientific responsibility and citizenship, recognizing the intricate interplay between scientific advancement and societal impacts. We will explore the historical and contemporary dimensions of scientific research, critically examining how social structures and inequalities shape scientific practices. Through engaging with diverse perspectives and social theories, students will cultivate the skills to navigate ethical dilemmas, advocate for inclusive research practices, and contribute meaningfully to the advancement of science for the betterment of society.  

Some Highlighted Classes you might finding interesting :
- Mercury Contents of Bay Area Waters
- Berkeley & Nuclear Research
- The Racial History of the Spirometer
- Hormonal Birth Control
- Mrs. Henrietta Lacks & HeLa Cells
enrollmentInformation: GRP 004 #21204 / Regular Academic Session / 1.00 units

title: TreeCal: Tree ID & Ecology
description: This class will provide an introduction to some of the common trees of California and some of the nonnative species planted on the Berkeley campus. We will go over characteristic traits of these species, their functions in ecosystems, and general tree identification skills that can be applied to other ecosystems. Beyond that, we aim to increase awareness about ecology, ecosystems, and people, including Indigenous land management, the history of California ecosystems, current land-management issues, and what trees/forests mean to people culturally and spiritually. This knowledge will contextualize the technical skills of tree identification, both socially and politically, and will help students develop a well-rounded appreciation for ecosystem-human interactions.

title: Chemistry of Cooking
description: This class aims to help learners understand chemical nature of food, cooking and cooking materials. An essential component is having the students work together to cook and appreciate the chemistry going on in their meals!
enrollmentInformation: We do an application and all applicants are reviewed and let know of their standing in the class ASAP. We take about 70 students!

title: Children's Literature and Illustration
description:
How does one go about starting a career in children’s literature?” Our DeCal is molded out of this question, and we will seek to find an answer under the guidance of these three pillars: Inspiration, Creation, and Publication. 

This is a project-based class where students will experience creating their own children's book and learn more about the publishing industry.

This DeCal is perfect for writers who dream of publishing a children's book, artists who want hands-on experience in illustrating a project, and anyone who just wants to have fun, learn something new and make their inner child shine through!
enrollmentInformation:
Enrollment is application-based. 

The application has been simplified from previous semesters and requires submission of a portfolio AND/OR completion of a writing/drawing prompt. There is no preference for completing one or the other, so please choose whichever option you are most comfortable with!

Applications will close on January 31st, and results will be sent via email on February 1st or 2nd.

title: Bending Literary Canon — A Critical Analysis of Avatar: The Last Airbender
description:
"It is important to draw wisdom from many different places. If we take it from only one place, it becomes rigid and stale." - Uncle Iroh

Does Avatar: The Last Airbender (ATLA) belong in the literary canon? Should it be counted among the classics—Romeo and Juliet, Pride and Prejudice, or The Tale of Genji—in terms of prestige, cultural significance, and artistic merit? Furthermore, can a children’s animated series constitute literature at all? In this course, we will bend the literary canon, examining ATLA with the same care, rigor, and curiosity typically reserved for traditional canonical texts.

Students will use the series as a text for critical, thematic, and sociopolitical analysis, evaluating how effectively the series addresses complex issues such as genocide, colonialism, and imperialism. They will learn about the real-world cultural and historical inspirations behind ATLA, and discuss how these elements are blended and reimagined within the series. Students will also engage in light-hearted fandom discourse, share “hot takes,” and dive into the series’ lore in depth.

Students should expect to spend approximately 2.5 hours per week on course activities. These include watching assigned episodes, participating in discussion posts, and reviewing supplementary materials such as video essays and scholarly articles that provide theoretical frameworks for interpreting the series.
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment is by application, open until February 1. Complete and submit the Google Form below. Notifications of acceptance will be sent promptly. Class begins February 4th. Yip Yip!

title: Introduction to Neurotechnology
description:
This course is a technical introduction to the field of neurotechnology. We introduce and dive deep into the various topics that neurotechnology encompasses, including but not limited to neuroscience, signal processing, machine learning, brain imaging methods, and data science.

Upon completion of this course, students can expect to have developed a deep understanding of the brain as a biological organ as well as a computational machine. Students will be able to draw upon various techniques from signal processing and machine learning (using Python) to gather and analyze brain data, as well as build interfaces between humans and computers. Lastly, we want our students to reflect upon the “big picture” of the emerging field of neurotech -- why does this field exist, what are its applications, and how does it change what it means to be human? 

Students will learn through in-person lectures by facilitators and guests, weekly homework assignments (including Jupyter notebooks), and a final project.
enrollmentInformation: Enroll using the CNN: 28130

title: Sage Mentorship
description:
The Sage Mentorship DeCal is correlated with The Sage Mentorship Project club. This DeCal is open for both new and current members. If you're not already in the club, please attend one of our infosessions on February 3rd or 4th at 8-9pm to learn more about what we do and how to join. You can follow our Instagram @sagementorship for updates! 

The Sage Mentorship DeCal is worth 1 unit. The class and mentoring portions of the course will run concurrently throughout the semester, encouraging students to directly apply the lessons learned in class to issues that come up while mentoring. This DeCal offers UC Berkeley students a unique opportunity to critically engage in the theory and practice of education and the mentorship process while simultaneously applying it. Ultimately, the DeCal aims to facilitate discussions about mentorship and education more broadly to best equip mentors with the tools to support their mentees.

This course will dive deep into education topics applicable today, discussing how each topic plays into mentorship and education. By the end of the course, we hope students can:
1. Develop the tools to form strong personal connections with the youth in our community. 
2. Think critically about the theory and practice of working with children. 
3. Learn and use pedagogical methods relevant to elementary-age students. 
4. Recognize the potential legal and ethical boundaries a mentor must navigate.
enrollmentInformation: No application required. First come, first serve.

title: Fermentation 101: Sustainability through Fermentation and Pickling
description: This course explores the science and culture of fermentation and pickling with a focus on sustainability. Students will learn to ferment and pickle various foods, including often-discarded parts like peels and scraps. We’ll examine the science, safety, and cultural history behind preservation, with hands-on practice throughout.
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment is first come first serve. The first class will be Wednesday, January 28, 2026. Enrollment will close on January 30, 2026.

title: Non Profit Poverty Alleviation
description: This course is focused on nonprofit organizations and their role in alleviating poverty in the global south. This class will allow students to be exposed to some of the current debates around various strategies for global poverty alleviation, as well as provide critiques of these strategies. The course will start by introducing the role of nonprofits in the development field, and explore characteristics of effective nonprofits versus harmful non-profit practices. We will then move into the methods of nonprofit change and their interactions with different actors. The class will then focus on the strategies and angles of addressing poverty with guest speakers and practitioners to expose students to the work and its challenges. The course will be primarily lectures with occasional activities for discussion. There will also be classes which pose discussion questions that will be discussed within small groups and then transition into a class brainstorming session.

title: Medicinal Nutrition
description:
This course focuses on nutrition as medical therapy to prepare students for a future in healthcare.
Students will learn preventative nutritional medicine, culinary methods, healthy living, and scientific
approaches uncovering the effects of nutrition in medicine. Students will listen to lectures, participate in
projects, complete at-home assignments, and participate in discussions to obtain a p/np grade in this
course. This course is open to any student interested in nutrition, medicine, well-being, and healthcare.
enrollmentInformation: The CCN is #29055 and enrollment is capped at 100 students.

title: The Evolution of American National Security Strategy (NSS) Post-Cold War (1991-Present)
description:
The period following the Cold War reshaped the international system and forced the U.S. to rethink how it is to prioritize and exercise national security. This course places students in the shoes of policymakers grappling with events such as the rise of China, the War on Terror, and Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Through simulations, guided discussions, and case studies, students will learn how to assess policy and craft new strategies. Students will practice strategic reasoning by analyzing second order effects and tradeoffs, skills needed to think broadly about challenges the U.S. faces in the coming decades. 
 
In terms of participation, this course expects weekly discussion posts, lively class participation, and completed readings from all its students.

title: The Housing Crisis!
description:
This student-led course will examine why there is a need for decent, affordable housing and
how this need can be addressed. The course analyzes frameworks—both failed ones from
the past, and a host of recent new ways of thinking from the present—as a way of
understanding the complexity of the issue. The course will feature guest speakers from
academia, government, and nonprofit organizations sharing unique perspectives on
housing. Students will participate in discussions and group activities to further reflect on
material presented by lecturers. While there is no final exam for this course, students will
demonstrate their knowledge through field work and a final group presentation.

title: Advances in Neuroscience Research
description: This course explores modern neuroscience research by introducing key experimental techniques and current discoveries. Through lectures, journal club discussions, and guest speakers, students learn how methods are used to address scientific questions and how to critically analyze primary research papers. The course culminates in a group project where students propose new studies or experimental follow-ups, helping them develop strong skills in interpreting, evaluating, and designing neuroscience research.

title: MD Mindspace
description: MD Mindspace is a student-led, 2-unit (P/NP) course that fosters mental health awareness, self-care, and resilience among future healthcare professionals. Through weekly discussion- and activity-based sessions—including speaker panels, mindfulness practices, journaling, and creative arts—students learn to recognize signs of burnout and stress while building sustainable self-care strategies. The course offers a supportive space for open dialogue, reflection, and community-building, with the goal of destigmatizing mental health in medicine and empowering students to thrive personally and professionally.
enrollmentInformation: Enroll through Enrollment Center in CalCentral with class number 29043.

title: Origins and History of Consciousness
description:
What makes things interesting? That is the question at the heart of this course. We will explore narrative, mythology, and politics to find fundamental patterns of human perception and behavior. This course will reinvent the way you view everything, from Marvel Movies to Trump's speeches. 

The course's only out-of-class assignment consists of conducting an analysis of any work of fiction, mythology, or political movement using tools taught in class.

title: UCBUGG: 3D Modeling and Animation
description:
Throughout this class, students learn how to 3D Model and Animate using the software Autdesk Maya. Students will learn how to create their own short 1-2 minute animated film, learning how to storyboard, model, rig, shade, animate, and render their work! No experience is required at all as we accept all levels of students having both a Basic and Advanced course. I encourage everyone to come to our info-session on Monday (1/26) from 7PM - 9PM or Friday (1/30) 7PM - 9PM, whichever time works best for you!

If you would like to see past student's work check out our YouTube channel:
https://www.youtube.com/@UCBUGG
enrollmentInformation: You must attend one of the info-sessions either on Monday 1/26 or Friday 1/30. After attending one of the info-sessions, you will be given details on how to officially apply into the DeCal!

title: Step Out of Overdrive
description:
Nowhere in life are we specifically taught how to manage our stress. We’ve accepted stress as a part of our daily lives as students and young adults, and sometimes equate success with stress. If we’re not stressed, often we don’t feel successful. Stress impacts every aspect of our lives: our health, relationships, education, etc.

In this Decal, we would like to introduce students to evidence-based breathing and meditation techniques, for them to choose to employ in their own lives. We would like to create conversation around different modes of stress management, and the link between stress and our forms of expression, i.e. our human values: service, creativity, happiness, leadership, authenticity, awareness, etc.

The final is a retreat that is mandatory, which will be held March 6th through 8th in Berkeley. If you cannot attend the retreat, sign up another semester.
enrollmentInformation: Application Process - We are looking for demonstrated interest and folks who can attend the retreat!

title: Foundations of Engineering Biology
description:
Synthetic biology is the engineering of organisms to create new abilities or redesign existing systems that will help solve problems in the fields of medicine, sustainability, manufacturing, agriculture and more. iGEM at Berkeley is a synthetic biology-focused club that provides students the opportunity to develop their knowledge of synthetic biology and participate in student-run, hands-on research projects.

Offered through Berkeley’s iGEM club, this course will guide students through experimental techniques of the cloning workflow and synthetic biology. Students will learn and conduct hands-on lab techniques such as PCR, primer design, gel electrophoresis, DNA cleanup, assembly, transformation, and sequencing. 

Over the course of the semester, students will complete our pP6 cloning workflow experiment, designed by Professor Anderson, to contribute to generating a library of uber-strength promoters. The final project will be a three-week long Design Challenge in which teams of students will perform literature search, conduct ideation, and present a project proposal to a panel of peers.

Students will learn the fundamental lab techniques required in synthetic biology and be able to apply that knowledge to conducting the cloning workflow. 

Specific skills:
- Pipetting 
- Primer design for cloning methods
- Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR)
- Gel electrophoresis
- DNA cleanup
- Assembly 
- Transformations
- Picking
- Mini-prep
- Sequencing
- Basic experimental data analysis techniques 
- Lab documentation skills


There will be a one-hour lecture along with a two-hour lab section each week. During lecture, students will learn about the concepts behind the following week’s lab, including how to conduct the specific experimental technique. The homework assignments for each week will be due before lab of the following week and will focus on the step of the experiment being done that week. During the lab, students will conduct the wet lab technique taught during the previous week’s lecture. Throughout the semester, students will work on a collaborative project to address a biological issue in the world using techniques/background information provided through this course. Weeks 7-10 will focus on a sequential cloning workflow in which every student must individually complete each technique in order.
enrollmentInformation:
To apply to the Foundations of Engineering Biology DeCal, please fill out the following form: https://forms.gle/JavYVXgB7xX7LUQr5
The deadline for this application is January 30th at MIDNIGHT. You will be notified if you are on the roster via email by the following Sunday along with the course and CCN information to enroll. Do NOT miss this email to avoid enrollment fees.

As a reminder, this DeCal is meant to introduce students to hands-on synthetic biology laboratory techniques involving the basic cloning workflow. Due to this, we expect you have a solid foundation from AP Biology, BIOENG 10/11, BIOLOGY 1A/1AL, exposure to lab work preferred, but not required. If you have past experience with these techniques, we highly encourage you to check out the application for our wet-lab experimental teams or sign up to be one of the leads!

title: Cancer 101: Introduction to Cancer and Molecular Therapeutics
description: Once described as the “Emperor of all Maladies,” cancer kills over 20,000 people every day worldwide. Despite the widespread impact of this disease, most people have little more than a basic idea of the workings of cancer. Did you know that cancer affects one in two men and one in three women in the United States? Or, that cases of skin cancer due to indoor tanning exceed cases of lung cancer due to smoking? Want to learn why taller women are more likely to get cancer? It’s about time that everyone knows a little something about cancer. If you are fascinated by this disease and want to learn the science behind it, enroll in Cancer 101!
enrollmentInformation: First Come, First Serve; no applications necessary

title: A Peoples Economy: Exploring Alternative Economics
description:
This course explores how non-capitalist economic structures can function in practice by examining real-world examples such as community land trusts, worker cooperatives, public finance systems, and alternative forms of exchange. Through these cases, students will investigate how communities, often those excluded from or exploited by mainstream capitalist systems, have come together to reclaim land, resources, and wealth, to reimagine what equitable and sustainable economic life can look like. The course examines these initiatives not only as economic experiments but as movements for dignity, self-determination, and justice. Students will also develop practical skills essential to the success of such systems, including group decision-making, stakeholder balancing, empathy, and effective communication.
Fitting at the intersection of economics, politics, urban studies, and community development, this course complements theory-based classes by focusing on applied, community-driven approaches to economic transformation. This class is largely structured to teach using story telling, discussions, and group activities to help foster learning through experiences rather than strictly lectures. It is aimed at students who want to think creatively about organizing economies around care, equity, and collaboration rather than extraction and profit. Students will leave with a deeper understanding of the possibilities and challenges of building alternative economies, as well as the analytical and interpersonal tools needed to participate in or design such systems themselves, skills valuable for future work in social innovation, public service, and community-based change. 
The course is discussion- and activity-based, emphasizing storytelling, group exercises, and reflective journaling. Students complete weekly reflection posts, participate actively in class, and collaborate on a final project that challenges students to reimagine economics
enrollmentInformation: This class has open enrollment!

title: Premed 101
description: Looking to learn how to be a successful Pre-med student at Cal, what it's like to be in medical school, and what a medical profession truly entails? Look no further...the PreMed 101 DeCal is a 1 unit P/NP class that will be offered in-person this Spring on Tuesday evenings from 6:30-8:00 PM to teach you about all things pre-med, from which classes to take, to crushing the MCAT, to what it's like to work in the medical field as a doctor, nurse, or PA! The majority of classes will feature presentations from guest speakers who work in a wide range of medical specialties, and they're eager to share their paths to medicine and current experiences in their career! The course's Facilitators hope to serve as your mentors both during and beyond your time in this class. Enroll in our course through CalCentral (Course Number: 22649). First class is Tuesday, February 3rd! Questions? Email the Facilitators at ucbpremed101@gmail.com!
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served

title: Soil-Seq: Environmental Health and Engineering Research
description:
Interested in health, STEM education research, or environmental engineering? This course aims to create a multi-disciplinary space for students to critically engage with the intersection between STEM education and environmental engineering from an application-centered perspective. Students will first learn about fundamental concepts of environmental surveillance, particularly within the context of monitoring increasing antibiotic resistance and emerging infectious diseases. At the same time, the class will highlight the nature of STEM education at the high school level and challenge students to discuss ways to improve the current state of scientific education with regards to community representation, subject engagement, and long-term retainment. This exploration will include a fieldwork research segment involving student participation in our environmental engineering outreach program, called SOIL-SEQ!

SOIL-SEQ is a hands-on education module for high school classrooms designed to engage students in a crowdsourcing effort to provide data to better understand the spread of bacterial antibiotic resistance through the environment. The program is designed to be taught over four sessions in high school science classrooms. Students learn about antibiotic resistance and environmental surveillance, collecting their own soil samples, and using portable DNA sequencing and bioinformatics to determine whether antibiotic resistant bacteria are present in the soil. Students analyze their results by using a bioinformatics pipeline for their soil DNA sequencing data, and ultimately contribute their results to a geographically linked database of antibiotic resistance in soil. The goals of this module are to get high school students excited about environmental engineering and STEM, as well as create a global database of antibiotic resistance genes found in local soil samples to help inform antibiotic resistance surveillance and prevention programs. Interested in enrolling? Use our code 35140 on CalCentral!
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served--to enroll, use our course code 35140 on CalCentral!

title: The Art of Taiko Drumming

sections.room: M: Upper Sproul Plaza, W: Dwinelle 179

description:
Curious about the art form of taiko? Want to learn how to play? Want to discuss its cultural background, and its presence in Asian American art today? Join us at our DeCal! In this 2-unit course, students will learn how to play taiko (a style of Japanese ensemble drumming) and study its history and impact in North America.The class will be taught in two portions: learning how to play the drum (2 hrs/wk) and the discussion (1 hr/wk). 

The playing portion covers hands-on learning in the playing techniques of taiko. Students will learn basic stance and hitting technique in two styles, naname and beta, as well as the oral tradition of song learning. We will also be teaching students how to confidently solo on the drum, which will be showcased in their final performance at the end of the semester. The performance will include the two songs that are taught over the course of the semester. 

The discussion portion will cover the historical and cultural significance of taiko in Japan, North America, and beyond. Students will be able to meet and talk with prominent members of the taiko community during guest lectures. We will also consider how place, ethnic identity, and gender, among other topics, have impacted taiko's development as an art form and are impacted by taiko today. This will also include the course homework, weekly reading assignments as well as three short papers. 

Attendance is a significant part of the final grade, with up to two unexcused absences will be allowed. Any more than two unexcused absences will result in a failing grade in the course. Excused absences are acceptable. 

You need NO prior experience and it is open to ALL! Join us for hands-on experience while having thorough discussions every week about the cultural and historical significance of the art form as it has evolved over time.

title: South Indian Cinema
description:
South Indian cinema has for many decades been a form of representing both dominant and oppositional political ideologies, and recently has been ruling over Indian cinema: the success of Pushpa, KGF, Kantara, and Ponniyin Selvan in the last few years alone show how incredibly central South Indian film is becoming to dialogues about film and Indian identity. This course will focus on four of the main industries of the south: the Tamil, Telugu, Malayalam, and Kannada film industries.

Discussion and Lecture will be in English, and movies will have English subtitles, so no previous knowledge required! There are no exams or homework, but attendance is required (with makeup opportunities), and there will be a final project. Check out our syllabus for more details!
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment will be decided by application only! Applications are due at 11:59pm on January 30th, and we'll reach out via email on January 31st to let you know if you've been admitted or waitlisted.

title: Beginner's Guide to the Universe
description:
This class teaches the conceptual foundations and frontiers of modern physics and astronomy without the typical mathematical barriers. This DeCal is designed for both non-majors and introductory-level physics and astronomy students. There are no math or physics prerequisites at all, just healthy curiosity and a willingness to ask questions! If you are an intended physics/astro major looking to get an early, accessible preview of advanced theoretical topics that you may come across in research — Or if you are a non-major interested in a light class to learn about the universe, this class is for you!

We will have many fun demos and small group discussions:

On the technical side we will cover: how chaos emerges from simple physical rules; how thermodynamic entropy provides for an arrow of time; Einstein's connection of gravity to spacetime; the spooky world of quantum mechanics; the lives of stars; the history of the universe; and scientists' centuries-long endeavor towards a unified picture of the fundamental forces.

On the humanities side we will discuss: how the development of physics has given both tremendous benefit and harm to human societies; and how our philosophies of physics are intertwined with our views of the natural world.

If interested, please sign up for 2 units P/NP.
enrollmentInformation: Open enrollment; first come, first serve

title: Modern Russian Cinema
description:
Aimed at students curious about Russian language and culture, this course provides an introduction to modern and contemporary Russian film through creative, interactive, and discussion-based methods. Through weekly film screenings, guided analysis, and collaborative exercises, students will explore themes concerning Russian and post-Soviet society, film aesthetics, notable directors and actors, as well as cultural and historical contexts.

The course emphasizes critical viewing and creative engagement rather than rote memorization or technical mastery. Students will be encouraged to analyze how and why films are made the way they are, with particular attention to production choices, visual motifs, and the potential social, political, and historical environments that shape cinematic storytelling. Ultimately, this DeCal aims to combine academic insight with community building and creative exploration, drawing more students into the vibrant field of Slavic studies.

The capstone of this DeCal will involve two creative projects (one individual and one collaborative) both inspired by the films and theories explored in class: 
1) The individual project will consist of a creative piece (short story, photo series, poem, collage, essay, drawing, or other medium) inspired by a theme or aesthetic encountered in the course. 
2) The group project will involve the production of a short film (~10 minutes in length), collaboratively written and produced by a team of classmates. 

Selected works from both projects will have the opportunity to be published to a dedicated section of Troika (the Slavic Department’s undergraduate journal) highlighting the artistic range and interpretive creativity of the class.

In addition to the above creative projects, short homework assignments will be given to prepare students for film screenings and concepts discussed in class. These assignments should be interesting, enrich what you take away from the class, and take no longer than 30 minutes.
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment: first come, first served, based on attendance at the first class on Thursday, January 29th.

title: Cult Classic Cinema
description: What is cult cinema? How is it defined? Characteristics of cult films range from being “so bad they’re good” to having cult-like fanbases to being underground or obscure. Amongst fans of cult cinema, it remains difficult to pinpoint an exact definition, as this film classification spans genres and decades. In this student-led Decal, we will study the history, social and cultural influence, and various subcategories of cult cinema in order to broaden our understanding and engage with discourse surrounding its many definitions. NOTE: Many of these films fall into the “cult” category because of their explicit nature. Students should be aware that these films contain some graphic content.

title: Making the Economy Better for Society
description:
Greed is good, says Adam Smith, an oft-quoted line in the field of economics. But Smith follows this with the lesser-cited statement: “humanity, justice, generosity, and public spirit are the qualities most useful to others.” So when did the well-being of the economy become at odds with the well-being of people? Why is it that, in today’s market economy of capitalism, big corporations flourish and the top 1% grow richer while the working class suffers? 

This Decal explores today’s greatest societal inequalities through the lens of both economics and sociology, unpacking “middle-out” economics (an alternative to top-down economics) through various case studies and special topics presented in episodes of Nick Hanaeur’s podcast “Pitchfork Economics.” 

Each week will be structured around a selected podcast episode (which feature additional guests like Ingrid Robeyns, Brad DeLong, etc.), which students will listen to before class. Students will also learn about the different schools of economic and sociological thought, with an emphasis on addressing the missing perspective of sociology in college economics classes. Students will critically analyze texts and podcast episodes through written responses and in-class discussions. 

Students will produce their culminating project over the course of the semester: a written and recorded podcast episode of their own that explores a related topic of their choice. The final project may be completed individually or in groups of 2-3 students.
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served. Open to all regardless of academic background.

title: Teaching to Remember: Engaging with Adults with Alzheimer's & Dementia
description:
A study of special topics that are not covered in depth in other courses in the department. Course objectives align with the goals of the CalTeach program, which include developing skills in STEM pedagogy; diversity, equity, inclusion and social justice in learning, and/or teacher preparation. 

Do you wonder how to make a difference in someone’s life while learning valuable teaching skills with a research-based approach? This course equips you with evidence-based teaching and memory enhancement techniques specifically designed for working with adults with Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and Dementia, as well as new technologies that support memory, communication, and care. Through interactive activities, exposure to tools such as cognitive apps and assistive devices, and volunteer experiences at an elder center, students will learn how pedagogy and innovation can work hand-in-hand to improve quality of life for individuals with AD. Prepare to integrate research findings, teaching practice, and technology as you gain a holistic understanding of Alzheimer’s and Dementia!
enrollmentInformation:
First come, first serve.
Course Code: 28046

title: Life Skills: Intro to Baking
description:
A puddle of inedible chocolate chip sludge that refuses to be pried from the pan, which is probably for the best. A cake whose texture can only be likened to a sponge: not in a good way. A gummy loaf of bread that even peanut butter and jelly cannot save. If these baking disasters are reminiscent of your own encounters with the oven, this course is for you.

In this course, we will be learning how to tackle a variety of baked goods, paying special attention to the fundamental techniques, and basic science behind ingredient selection, which will allow you to leave this class confident in your ability to create satisfying desserts worthy of being shared.

Each week, you will be expected to complete a bake and do a short write up on that bake as if writing a food blog. The bake will be determined by the material covered in class.
enrollmentInformation: Please fill out the application!

title: Impacts of the Showgirl: Taylor Swift
description:
Taylor Swift. A figure who almost instantly sparks polarizing opinions, was TIME’s 2023 Person of the Year, four time album of the year winner at the Grammys, and performed in front of 10 million people over the course of the biggest stadium tour in history. This three-unit DeCal will dissect how Swift’s singer-songwriter talent, combined with personal branding, has had outsized impacts on political discourse and public policy. Swift’s novel approach to re-record her old music and regain ownership has reshaped entertainment copyright law, and the complications with Ticketmaster during her Eras Tour have led to FTC antitrust investigations. The course will present students with critical questions about what actions are expected by billionaires, cultural celebrities, and artists. Whether you love, hate, or have somehow never heard of Taylor Swift, this course will discuss nuanced issues in culture and public policy using Swift as the lens.

The course will have three papers as well as a full class mock trial/debate and a final project. There will be one 1.5h lecture per week on Wednesdays from 6:30pm-8:00pm

Enrollment is first come, first serve and the course number is: 30287 (PUBPOL 198 005)
enrollmentInformation: First-come, first-served

title: Next Generation Leaders: BTS
description:
This course will focus on Korean boy group, BTS, analyzing their influence through an academic lens. Some of the content we will cover includes the artists’ history, image, and philanthropy. The class attempts to define their impact by discussing globalization and media portrayals. Students will be able to reflect on their own perspectives regarding the intersection of culture, language, and identity by observing BTS’ rise to fame in both the Eastern and Western hemispheres. 

In-class sessions will consist of lectures, screenings, and group discussions. Lectures will focus on introducing material, while the screenings will supplement the material. 

Out-of-class assignments will consist of weekly reflections that require analyzing concepts discussed in class. Additionally, the readings due each week will aid in preparation for the following class. 

Attendance is highly crucial as it is where most of the learning and discussions will take place and it is what the assignments are based upon. Participation depends on engagement in class and doing the readings.
enrollmentInformation: Applications are now open! Please make sure to RSVP and attend one of our two infosessions as well!

title: Intro to DataViz in R
description: We’re surrounded by data visualizations — in the news, on social media, and everywhere online — but how many of them are actually telling the truth? This DeCal introduces the grammar of graphics using R and ggplot2, teaching students how to create, recreate, and critically evaluate data visualizations they see in the real world. You’ll learn how design choices shape interpretation, build clear and compelling plots from scratch, and develop essential data literacy skills to spot misleading charts and communicate insights responsibly.
enrollmentInformation: Please fill out the application form below and we will send you a permission number.

title: Navigating Hawaiian Language & Culture
description:
Hawaiʻi is the 8-island chain in the Pacific, home to some of the greatest voyagers in the world and to a reviving culture and language today. This student-facilitated, 2-unit course at UC Berkeley is an introduction to the culture, history, and language of Hawaiʻi.  


This class is designed to bring insight to the Hawaiian perspective and spark critical thinking for conversation about current issues. There will be an emphasis on unlearning the colonizer’s history and relearning Hawaiʻi’s complex history through the works, experiences, and expertise of Kānaka Maoli scholars and activists. This will include discussions of Aloha ʻĀina and the intersubjective relationship between kānaka and our land. Additionally, we will discuss the history of the language from its outlaw to its revitalization and perpetuation today. We are our ancestor's greatest dreams. We speak their languages so we will be able to converse when we join them. 


The ultimate goal of this course is to gain a basic understanding of ʻŌlelo Hawaiʻi (Hawaiian language), perpetuate Hawaiian culture, and discuss how Hawaiʻi is still undergoing colonization. Feel free to explore this site to learn more about this course!
enrollmentInformation: Please send a short email (1 or 2 sentences) to stacyc123@berkeley.edu or jadakuulei@berkeley.edu sharing why you would like to take the course. Requests are approved on a first come, first serve basis.

title: (Mathematical) Modeling in Biology
description:
This decal is meant for students interested in learning how to apply mathematical ideas and tools, in particular those of systems biology, to better understand and steer living systems. Unlike most other biologists (or high-energy experimentalists) who break things apart to catalog and study their parts, systems biologists develop mathematical models of the biological 'circuits' that exist at cellular, tissue-level, and even ecological scales. Doing so provides (i) a better understanding of natural biological systems and (ii) rules for how to design new synthetic ones.

The course is designed to be manageable for anyone comfortable with calculus and ODEs. No previous biology background is required (we'll learn on the go). People who might enjoy this class include: biologists who'd like to apply mathematics to model their subject of interest; physicists/mathematicians interested in learning about non-traditional modeling methods (such as control theory and nonlinear dynamics); and anyone else interested in learning some of the ways cells and animals thrive in rapidly shifting environments.

Last note: the course is seminar-style, meaning classes are split into short lectures and student presentations on problems from the previous week's homework.
enrollmentInformation: Class #24440 on CalCentral. No permission needed to enroll.

title: A Critical Understanding of Public Health
description:
The purpose of this course is to engage students in active and informed discussion regarding the interdisciplinary field of Global Health. The course will explore and explain the various social, political, and cultural underpinnings of health issues. Understanding the functional tools behind delivering sustainable health care and ensuring equity in treatment involves examining global health through the frameworks of its individual disciplines. Simple terms such as “aid” and “development” need to be examined in the context of their colonial roots as well as their place in the 21st century. 

          This course involves studying the work of current pioneers in global health as well as the various disciplines that contribute to it, including but not limited to anthropology, social medicine, sociology, health economics, and political science. Social literature and history will be taught alongside current medicine and research. Each work will be examined with the goal of ultimately equipping students with the tools to investigate current health care and development models from a holistic standpoint and encourage an understanding that will bolster their contributions to social justice in health care.

Enroll in CalCentral using course code #30694

title: From Tokenism to Stardom: Identity and Power in American TV (1989–2025)
description: This course critically examines the representation of marginalized identities—particularly women of color—in American television sitcoms and dramas from 1989 to 2025. We explore how colorism, intersectionality, and social media influence character portrayals, narrative development, and cultural impact. By focusing on sitcoms and dramas, the class examines how colorism, gender, class, and race intersect in casting, narrative, and character development. Drawing on cultural sociology, intersectionality theory, and media studies, we examine how visual storytelling both reflects and reshapes norms surrounding identity, particularly among Gen Z. Students will learn to critically analyze the evolving politics of representation using established evaluative frameworks such as the Bechdel, Waithe, Ko, Villalobos, and Mori Tests. Weekly discussions and assignments encourage students to formulate evidence-based critiques, assess structural power dynamics, and connect media representations to broader social institutions and inequalities. The final weeks center on how tokenism has evolved into stardom and what this transformation reveals about society, media, and self-identification. The course is divided into thematic sections and concludes with an analysis of the shift from tokenism to stardom in television culture.
enrollmentInformation: First-come, first-serve

title: Tokenism to Stardom: Identity and Power in American TV (1989–2025)

title: Exercise is Medicine
description:
Exercise is Medicine-On Campus (EIM-OC) is an effort created in collaboration with UHS, Rec Sports, School of Public Health and Dept. of Phys Ed. Through this course, you will learn the benefits of daily exercise, explore programs on campus to help you stay active, and discover careers in healthcare that promote physical fitness and wellbeing.

Each class is unique and interactive, building upon the connections that exercise is optimal for health, is integral in the prevention and treatment of many medical conditions, and should be regularly assessed and included as part of health care. Learn from a variety of industry professionals, including physicians, physical therapists, registered dietitians, and more!
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment is on a first come, first serve basis. You must enroll via Enrollment Center in CalCentral— you may find it by searching with the Class # (30695). If you are waitlisted, please attend the first class on February 3rd.

title: Introduction to Go
description:
The Introduction to Go course teaches basic techniques of Go. he game of Go encourages
critical thinking, problem-solving, and strategy planning, carrying its academic values in math as
well as other domains. For example, developing strategic moves and memorizing and utilizing
different “Josekis” (fixed patterns and variations on the game board) require substantive
analyzing, calculative, and memorizing abilities. Students will learn strategies for playing complete games by the end of the course. They will also have developed a solid foundation in fundamentals and will be equipped to continue their study and practice of the game.
enrollmentInformation: The enrollment process comes with a first-come, first-serve basis. For the other students beyond capacity, we will notify the students by email and put them on the waitlist. We will update the waitlist if people choose to drop the course.

title: Demystification of the Theatrical World
description: This course aims to provide an accessible introduction to the world of theater. For those who might feel intimidated by the seemingly large threshold to join, for those who want to get involved but simply don’t know where to start, or for those who want a non-exhaustive introduction without having to commit 4 units to a TDPS class: we are for you! This course will consist of a 2-hour lecture once a week, taught both by facilitators and guest lecturers, as well as readings connected to the topic of the week. The class will culminate in a hands-on project in which students will have the opportunity to have experience dealing with any of the aspects of theater we have covered in our course. Students will also learn about different opportunities on campus, for any aspect of theater.
enrollmentInformation: Application required, all are welcome.

title: Global Public Health Disparities + Research
description:
In this 2 unit course, you and your team will choose a UNICEF Sustainable Development Goal and a country to explore a real-world health issue. Together, you’ll dig into root causes, design a creative action plan, and present your solution at an end-of-semester symposium. Along the way, learn from guest speakers who have made this real world change firsthand. 

Build skills, make an impact, and have fun doing it — open to all majors!
enrollmentInformation: Students accepted on a first come first serve basis.

title: Policy Review @ Berkeley
description: Policy Review @ Berkeley is a student-led, interdisciplinary journal designed for students of all academic disciplines to reflect on and write about the policy implications in their fields of interest. This journal’s mission is to make policy easily accessible and understandable for undergraduates from varying disciplines and personal backgrounds. The Policy Review @ Berkeley DeCal course is designed to help students build academic writing, policy-making, and analysis skills through comprehensive writing and thought-provoking simulations.
enrollmentInformation: Please fill out this application to be considered for this Decal: https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSd4uNkEZ5EtB2Yh_iGjCZXdix6XvnyVoPpe1kxPmh3NnB6UXg/viewform?usp=header

title: Digital Subcultures with Anthropomorphic Identities
description:
This course will examine digital subcultures with a specific focus on anthropomorphic art, identity expression, and the evolution from the wider online community. Through a combination of lectures, discussions, and group activities, students will gain a better understanding of the anthropomorphic identity. Students will explore how the digital landscape fostered such a community and how it intertwined with many other communities throughout history.

This class requires completion of readings, short homework assignments, and a final project due on the last day of class.

title: Reading Danmei
description:
This class introduces students to danmei, a literary genre of male-male romance that was initially inspired by Japanese yaoi/Boys' Love (BL) cultures but soon developed its own politics, aesthetics and feelings within the broader context of Chinese digital culture. We will closely read danmei texts from the early 2000s through the 2020s to trace the evolution of the genre over the past two decades and examine its key themes. Throughout the course, we will consider the roles of realism, the modes of love, and the representations of feelings in danmei, asking what modes of critical thinking these texts enable – both within and beyond the realm of literature. To better situate these texts, we will also engage with contemporary discussions in queer studies and neurodiversity studies.

This class is geared towards students with some prior exposure to the genre, though those with little familiarity are also welcome. Class sessions combine lecture segments with seminar-style discussion. Evaluation will be based on participation, completion of assigned readings and weekly reading responses, and a final presentation on a danmei text of the student’s choice.

Instruction will be in English, while reading will include materials in Simplified Chinese and English, and occasionally in Traditional Chinese.
enrollmentInformation: Interested students should fill in the google form on course website (https://readingdanmei.notion.site).

title: Percy Jackson and the Olympians
description:
The objective of this class is to read through and analyze Rick Riordan’s first series: Percy Jackson and the Olympians and discover what has made the series a Young Adult classic. We will read through the full first series and read excerpts of the myths as they are recorded in classical literature. We will also investigate how Percy Jackson interacts with our socio-cultural beliefs with the help of supplemental theory, such as disability theory and gender theory, to understand what makes Percy Jackson such a captivating and unique hero.

Each book will be read over a period of two weeks, allowing ample time for discussion and reading. Each week there will also be supplemental readings to help students make more informed analyses of the series and how it makes use of classical myths. We will also have a week to discuss the controversial movie adaptation.

There are three components of the overall grade: participation, attendance, and a final project. Each student is expected to complete the readings and participate in the form of biweekly discussion posts and reading quizzes. Two excused absences are allowed and additional ones can be made up with extra credit or a makeup assignment. The final project is mandatory to pass but it is intended to be a fun activity where students can present on any topic they want as long as it is related to the course.

The course begins February 4th and is every Wednesday at 6:30-8 pm in Dwinelle 251.
enrollmentInformation: There is an application to enroll in the class because of the high interest in the course. The deadline is January 30th at 11:59 pm, and students will be notified by February 2nd that they are selected for the course. The first class will be February 4th at 6:30-8 pm in Dwinelle 251.

title: Topics in Sexual Health
description:
Students will be educated in pleasure anatomy, safer sex methods, kink topics, sexual pleasure techniques, communication, LGBTQ+ sex, sex with disabilities, and other topics surrounding the sexual human body. These topics are intended to educate anyone and everyone on the pleasure aspects of the sexual body, and are selected with the intention to give an overview of the multiple complexities surrounding sex. This course is designed to be a safe space for judgment-free education and discussion for students to engage in. 

For more info, email: Calsexperts@gmail.com
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment is on a first come, first serve basis.

title: Classical Music Herstory
description:
Over the past few decades, women have gained warranted recognition within their respective fields. Female composers are no exception. Constantly operating within a man’s world, they offer a unique perspective within the Western Classical Music canon. Yet their music is technically indistinguishable from their male counterparts. This class attempts to reconcile these conflicting facts by recognizing the hardships that female composers underwent while simply using their compositions as examples of various stylistic periods, as would a typical music history class with the “Bach through Brahms” curriculum. 

This course acts as a survey of the lives and music of composers in the Pre-Baroque, Baroque, Classical, Romantic, and Modern Periods. Major innovators to be studied in depth include Clara Schumann, Ethel Smyth, and Undine Moore.

Through lectures and guided listening of pieces, students will learn class material. During class time, students will participate in group discussions to build connections between class material and any prior knowledge of music theory and history, as well as to allow space for sharing personal thoughts regarding the music. After lectures, students will be expected to demonstrate understanding of class material through self-chosen assignments. Lectures will be taught in-person.
enrollmentInformation: First come first served

title: Period Party!
description: Hey there! This is a course designed to provide education and awareness on various aspects of the female reproductive cycle that are incredibly lacking in our current education system. Throughout this interdisciplinary decal you will learn about you and/or your loved ones' reproductive health mainly from a biological standpoint but also in the context of culture, mental and physical health, and poverty. You will learn about not only the anatomy and physiology involved in the female reproductive cycle but also health concerns and disorders, inequitable access to menstrual products, and even holistic approaches to menstrual symptoms through interactive activities, workshops, guest lecturers, Q/A's, cycle tracking, and a yoga/meditation class.
enrollmentInformation: Fill out this interest form for enrollment details!

title: American Red Cross: Decal
description:
This course examines the American Red Cross as a model of humanitarian leadership, emergency management, and community service in the United States and abroad. Students will explore the organization’s origins, mission, and evolution within the broader context of public health, disaster relief, and volunteer mobilization. 

Through weekly lectures, simulations, and collaborative projects, the course investigates how the Red Cross coordinates large-scale responses to crises, manages blood and biomedical services, supports military families, and advances education in health and safety.

Students will engage with ethical questions surrounding humanitarian intervention, equity in disaster response, and the challenges of operating within complex political and social environments. Opportunities for field-based learning and Red Cross certification in CPR, First Aid, or Disaster Preparedness will be available.

By the end of the course, students will have developed a deeper understanding of how humanitarian organizations function, how leadership is exercised in times of crisis, and how they can apply these principles to their own professional and civic pursuits.

title: Electrify Cal: Clean Energy Campus
description:
This course will provide a comprehensive overview of the history and future of the Clean Energy
Campus Project and environmentalism at UC Berkeley. It will consist of lectures, guest speakers
(administration, professors, student groups, etc.), and walking tours of the cogeneration plant and
future Clean Energy Campus site. This course is intended for all students interested in clean
energy and sustainability, and assumes no prior knowledge. By the end of this course, everyone
will walk away with a strong understanding of the Clean Energy Campus project, student
environmental activism, and environmentalism as a whole. You will all be Clean Energy Campus
project experts, ready to tackle any "when, why, how" questions about the project. It will also
serve as a stepping stone to integrating yourself into the environmental scene at Cal.

title: Re-Thinking the War on Drugs
description:
Re-Thinking the War on Drugs is an interdisciplinary, intersectional Decal that seeks to challenge prevailing misconceptions about drugs: what they are, who uses them (and why), and what policies/practices most effectively foster healthier relationships with substances—at individual and societal scales. We will critically analyze America's history of drug criminalization and mass incarceration, examine rhetoric around addiction/recovery, and introduce conceptual frameworks and practical methods of harm reduction that seek to respond to widespread addiction and social-racial inequities.

Classes will be held on Wednesdays from 5-630PM and will consist of short lectures, guest presentations, and, primarily, group discussions of course topics. We (the facilitation team) strive to create a horizontally structured classroom environment where students can guide the way that class time is spent and course engagement is measured.

title: Intro to Compost and Vermiculture
description: Through lectures, workshops, and a field trip or two, this decal provides a broad, hands on introduction to the world of compost. We look at different types and scales of managed decomposition, the science and history of compost, learn how these topics fit into larger systems, and see them in action through home-composting workshops! Come join us!
enrollmentInformation: This DeCal is open to everyone! Enroll with this course number: #29034

title: Brand Identity and Graphic Design (BIGD)
description:
This course is an in-depth exploration of what branding is and how it works, through the eyes of a designer. Each student will come up with a brand concept for a company/business that's imaginary or real. Students will cultivate skills of conceptualizing and visualizing and learn the basic skills of graphic design as a means to make those ideas a reality. The course focuses on design as a tool to communicate. Students will learn about the conceptual elements of branding, and how to use graphic design tools like Adobe Illustrator and Photoshop as a valuable skill set that will help them in the future.

Classes will be held weekly on Tuesdays from 5:30-7pm, which will that'll include in-depth lectures, critique sessions, collaborative work time, and workshops facilitated by instructors!
enrollmentInformation:
Enrollment is by application, which are open January 20th and close February 2nd. You will be notified of your acceptance by February 4th. So apply today for Spring 2026! :)

Applicants will complete and submit a Google Form of questions, which can be found on our website (www.bigddecal.com) and through our Instagram bio (@bigddecal).

title: The Madness of March: Stats, Strategy, and History
description: This is a course that explores the NCAA tournament through probability, analytics, and decision making in the context of college basketball! We’ll break down brackets, upsets, and predictions using real data (no prior basketball knowledge required). If you have an affinity for combining sports and analytics (or want a fun, applied way to see math in action), this class is for you! Come play the bracket, learn to gamble, test the math, and embrace the madness!

title: Transit!
description:
ENROLLMENT IS FIRST COME, FIRST SERVED, NO APPLICATION! Enroll yourself in CalCentral, class #21496
~~~
Prior to your time here at Cal, how did you get around? How did the people around you get around?

This answer changes from person to person—or does it? In reality, with roughly 77% of the country driving to work, there’s a good chance that your answer involves a car in some form. Car dependency is a facet of life here in America, and it has been this way for decades.

That being said, things are changing. The younger generations have been overall using transit more than prior generations, along with delaying getting their driver’s license. Add in climate change and the impact the personal vehicle has had on accelerating it, we need solutions now. That being said, what should the solutions be? How should we go about picking the means that best fits the problem? Sure, at the state level we have SB 375 directing metropolitan planning organizations to reduce overall vehicle miles traveled, but what exactly could that entail?

It is my hope that through this course and the discussions we will have in class, you will gain the ability to critically look at transit systems and understand the context in which they exist. We will largely do this by looking at different transit systems, in addition to discussing technologies that they may employ, ranging from systems to planning technologies.
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served! Enroll yourself in CalCentral, class #21496

title: MEDLIFE
description:
MEDLIFE stands for Medicine Education and Development for Low Income Families
Everywhere, and is a national non-profit organization dedicated to promoting medicine,
education, and development in underprivileged communities in Latin America and Tanzania. As
part of their attempt to bring healthcare to rural areas, MEDLIFE provides the opportunity for
students to travel to these communities and provide assistance by working in temporary medical
clinics. While our course mainly prepares students for the medical brigade in the summer, this
course will also serve to introduce students to topics in global health and prepare them to be
global health leaders in the future. The course will focus on two major topics: 1) the importance
of global health awareness and sustainability in health, and 2) medical and conversational
Spanish skills that can enhance the experience of working with doctors, patients, and other
members of these communities. In order to accomplish these goals, the course will meet once a
week, each meeting lasting 2 hours.
