title: The Art of You: Intersections of Psychology, Creativity, & Expression
description:
This course is designed to help you gain insight into how your inner world - your emotions, thoughts, & experiences - shapes your artistic process. Through a psychological lens, we will delve into the nature of creative blocks & introspect on how past experiences can hinder your expressive flow. You will engage in holistic, contemplative practices to transform inner obstacles, such as fear & self-doubt, into sources of inspiration. If you are looking to better understand your emotional landscape & channel it into authentic expression, welcome.

In-person, discussion-based, and experiential course. Because the course is built on cultivating trust, presence, and courage, weekly in-person attendance is required (outside of emergencies). Assignments include weekly journaling/creative practices, 3–4 discussion boards, one reflection paper, and a final creative offering during the semester.

title: Ethics of AI - Thirteen discussions we should’ve had yesterday. CNN:  25651
description:
AI is everywhere, quite truly ubiquitous. Yet how much do we really know about it and its potential impacts on society? Have we talked enough about the ethical implications?

Enroll via Philos 198 on Cal Central - CNN:  25651

This course is about starting those overdue discussions, and asking ourselves:
“What exactly is (and isn’t) AI, what do we really mean when we use those initials, are we all talking about the same thing?”; “How aware are we of the bias inherent in AI and how we could/should address it?”; “Will the incessant manipulation we’re all already exposed to get better or worse under the influence of ever-evolving AI? What are the ethical implications there? How responsible are we for our own predicament? How much responsibility do we need to take?”; “What future are we ‘permitting’ for our young people? What impact, realistically, will AI have on the workforce and their prospects for employment and education? Is that ethical? Is it avoidable/addressable or is it already too late?”;

Join us for informative background, as well as lively interaction, in which there are no right or wrong answers, only discussions we all need to have - and should have had yesterday.

Grades will be Pass/Not Pass, and will be based upon attendance (1 to 2 classes may be missed with good reason, no more) as well as participation (including selection of discussion topics as assigned and introduction of valid and interesting points to the discussions).
facultySponsorName: Dr. Wesley H. Holliday, Professor of Philosophy
enrollmentInformation: All welcome - Enroll via Philos 198 on Cal Central - CNN:  25651

title: Psychology in the Cold War: Case Studies (PS 198)
description:
This course will focus on exploring various psychological theories such as the web of belief and bandwagoning, as well as case studies of Cold War events such as the Red Scare and Vietnam War. We will begin with an overview of psychological theories and their connection to international politics in the first half of the course. We will then move on to several case studies from the Cold War and examine each in the context of psychological theories. 

There are short weekly readings or videos that are the basis of each week's discussion. There is a final presentation to demonstrate each student’s understanding of the connection between their choice of a psychological theory and historical concept.
enrollmentInformation: First come first serve enrollment

title: Fashion x Data Science
description:
Fashion x Data Science is an upper division DeCal course designed to introduce how data science concepts are used within the fashion industry by balancing technical skills with practical applications. Topics build upon each other weekly alongside small, focused projects. Students form teams to complete a capstone project as the final assessment. The top projects will be selected to present to a panel of industry experts and may receive the opportunity for follow-up conversations outside of class. Founders and senior operators from notable organizations (ex. Poshmark confirmed! are expected to participate as guest speakers and/or final project judges during the semester.

Basic familiarity with Python is helpful but not required. The course prioritizes creative thinking and real-world practicality over advanced coding. Attendance is required.
enrollmentInformation: Submit a brief application by Jan 30 - applications will be accepted on a rolling basis, so apply sooner rather than later

title: Small Press Publishing: Berkeley Fiction Review
description:
The Berkeley Fiction Review is a student-run, ASUC-sponsored, annual publication that publishes artwork and 12-15 short stories from around the world. We publish innovative short stories from every genre. Established in 1981, BFR is the second-oldest literary journal on campus. In the spring, we will publish Issue 46!

As members of this DeCal, students read, discuss, and vote on submissions for publication. In addition, members have the chance to have their own work published through our Student Story Week. Occasionally, we have guest speakers from the creative writing or publishing fields. As a student in the class, you will play an integral role in the production of the journal from reading and voting on stories for publication, completing Reader’s Reports, and completing a personal project varying from opportunities to get further involved in the creation of our issue such as editing, design, or writing for our magazine!

Reading List
40 (1 unit) or 80 (2 units) short story submissions (averaging 15 pages each story) published on bCourses.

2-4 short stories per week for in-class small group discussion.
enrollmentInformation:
IMPORTANT NOTE: Submitting our enrollment form DOES NOT guarantee a permission code. Please make sure to attend our info session and ensure that your attendance is accounted for. We will only be able to enroll a maximum of 50 students who follow these steps accordingly. 

ADDITIONALLY: Since the publication is run through a DeCal, there is a limit on the number of staff members each semester. Therefore, enrollment will be decided based on applications, which will be assessed for enthusiasm and interest.

Our info session will be held on January 29, 2025, from 6:30 - 8pm, in Wheeler 204, where we will give an overview of the course and answer any questions. Attendance at our info session is MANDATORY for enrollment in the class.

Further meetings following the info session are expected to meet in-person at the same location (Wheeler 204) at the same time on Thursdays from 6:30 - 8pm.

title: Public Health 101: An Interdisciplinary Dive
description:
This DeCal is an interactive, interdisciplinary Decal focusing broadly on problems in local and global health. Course topics include an overview of healthcare in America and healthcare internationally, an introduction to analyzing and mapping public health data, climate change’s connection to public health, etc. In addition to introducing students to a wide array of public health issues, we will also dive into some of the solutions that have been implemented and will consider policies that have attempted to improve health in California, the U.S, and beyond.

The course will include collaborations with the MRI organization on campus for an experiential activity in which students will have the opportunity to volunteer at medical surplus redistribution organizations if they wish to do so to take their learnings beyond the classroom. The class itself will include guest lecturers coming in and will be discussion based.

Public health is formally defined as the science of protecting the safety and improving the health of communities through education, policy-making, and research for disease and injury prevention. Our DeCal aims to educate UC Berkeley students with an interest in public health, domestically and internationally in bioethics, and in particularly relevant topics, like international pandemics. Where possible, we hope to provide experiential learning opportunities, like the distribution of medical supplies and first aid training enable learning inside and outside f the classroom. 
Our course will foster students’ abilities to think critically about the material we will be presenting them with by assigning weekly reflections touching on the information presented in their class. We will also strive to build upon students’ prior knowledge of healthcare and introduce them to systemic issues pertaining to healthcare, so they may broaden their perspectives on the field of medicine and overcome their preconceived notions concerning healthcare as a whole. 

No prior knowledge is needed! This is a critical discourse class introducing students to topics related to global health disparities. The goal is to educate students to make more informed decisions regarding healthcare and healthcare policy as well as introduce them to global policy development.

title: Palestine: A Settler Colonial Analysis
description: This 2 unit course will examine key historical developments that have taken place in Palestine, from the 1880s to the present, through the lens of settler colonialism. Our discussions will focus in particular on the following questions: What new insights and perspectives emerge from a settler colonial analysis? What is the source of contemporary conflicts over land and political power in Palestine? What connections have scholars drawn between Zionism and settler colonialism? What might decolonization mean in this context, and how might it open the possibility for justice and equality for all peoples in the region? Our analyses will utilize a comparative approach with an emphasis on scholarship in settler colonial studies. Class time will be devoted to lecture, documentaries, and discussion about these issues and the relevant scholarly literature.
enrollmentInformation: Please fill out the google form below by the deadline and you will receive an email with further instructions.

title: Oral and Dental Health
description: The overall goal of the course is to bring awareness to the importance of proper oral hygiene and its relation to disease. Specifically, this dental health course will discuss oral hygiene, preventive measures, dental procedures and treatments, oral diseases, new dental technology through interactive activities, the introduction of 5-6 specialties within dentistry, as well as the process of applying to dental school.

title: Intersection of Politics and Technology
description:
This course explores the intersection of politics and technology, offering you a deep dive into the profound impact of technology on governance and society, and vice-versa. Throughout this course, we will analyze the ethical, social, cultural, and political implications of key topics such as tech regulation, algorithmic decision-making, data security and governance, AI, and more.

Through the careful examination of real-world case studies, you will gain a broad understanding of how technological advancements shape and influence policy-making processes and why educating others about such topics is important to create a more informed and equitable society in the face of rapidly growing technology.
enrollmentInformation: Enrol directly on CalCentral! First come first serve.

title: Introduction to Skincare
description: This course takes a science-based, research-driven approach to skincare, introducing students to a critical and evidence-based framework for analyzing skincare products. Each week, we will learn how to interpret ingredient lists, evaluate what makes a product effective or ineffective, distinguish between quality formulations and marketing-driven packaging, and determine which products are best suited to individual skin needs. Attendance is mandatory. The course includes one midterm project and a final project, both designed to apply course concepts by curating a personalized, science-backed skincare routine!
enrollmentInformation: First come, first serve!

title: Introduction to Biotech and Research Recruiting
description:
Healthcare and Business are two rapidly developing fields, and the intersection of the two is an emerging industry more valuable by the day. “Breaking in” can seem daunting at first, but through the comprehensive introduction this course provides, students will be prepared to enter the vast world of healthcare business from scratch. Students will progress through four modules that cover the broad buckets of healthcare business, learning through lectures, practical assignments, and guest lectures. The four modules encompass recruiting, healthcare consulting, biotechnology entrepreneurship, and
healthcare administration. No prior experience is required.

Students will be evaluated throughout the course based on their performance in the below grade buckets. 1 on 1 check-ins will be conducted at the midpoint of the course (Weeks 7 and 8) where instructors will both provide feedback on overall course performance and receive feedback on how the course can be improved going forward. Attending office hours throughout the course is encouraged, regardless of but especially if students are struggling.

Grading Policy (P/NP)
70 points to pass
Minimum of 50% points per category required to pass
Attendance and Participation (40 points)
Class Assignments / Homework (40 points)
Final Project (20 points)
enrollmentInformation:
Please fill out this form to apply for our Decal. This is not a competitive application, it is only intended to gauge genuine interest. Write a honest response without AI, and you will most likely be permitted to enroll. Thank you!

This form is open until Thursday February 5th at 11:59pm. The first day of the DeCal will be Tuesday February 10. When accepted, more information will be sent out.

title: Dream Logic: The Science and Storytelling of the Subconscious
description: This decal explores the meeting point between the science of dreaming and the art of storytelling. We will study how dreams, memory, and imagination intertwine to shape creative thought, drawing on short readings from neuroscience, psychology, and literature. Texts by writers such as Borges, Murakami, and Toni Morrison will be paired with accessible scientific works on the mechanics of REM sleep and the subconscious mind. Students will keep personal dream journals and short weekly reflections that connect their own experiences with the course material. The class balances critical discussion with creative experimentation, inviting students to approach dreaming as both a subject of inquiry and a tool for artistic expression.
enrollmentInformation: Submit the interest form ASAP to enroll, we have a class capacity of 18 students. If people drop the course after the first wave of enrollment, we will email those who weren't enrolled at first if they're still interested and have a second wave of enrollment.

title: Introduction to Figma
description: Through this student-run course, you will learn the mechanics of Figma, basic design theory, and unconventional ways to apply the skills you pick up during the semester. Although Figma is known for its UI/UX design capabilities, you can leverage it for anything that you can imagine!
enrollmentInformation: Application

title: Art for Animation
description:
This course is intended for students who are passionate about visual storytelling and want to learn about the animation pre-production pipeline (i.e. storyboarding, concept art, character design, etc.). Students will also be introduced to basic frame animation through exercises and homework.

Because this course provides a lot of useful information for animation, students are encouraged to take this course before (or concurrently with) UCBUGG: 3D Modeling and Animation DeCal or Center of New Media 190 (CNM 190)/Computer Science 194 (CS 194).

Classes are split into lectures with visual demonstrations of techniques or programs, and group critique time for student assignments. Both facilitators and students participate in the group critiques.

By the end of this course, students will have a concept art portfolio and original animatic.

Homework and project submissions will be graded on effort, level of understanding of weekly content as demonstrated by the student, and timely uploading of their assignments to their online portfolio.

Students will be submitting their assignments weekly via a google form.
 
If a student already has an online portfolio or website, they are free to use their existing website to display and submit their homework for the course.

Art is time consuming, and students are expected to invest a good portion of time (3 hours minimum each week) outside of class completing homework.
enrollmentInformation:
Students will be submitting their application via a google form. There will be a drawing test with several given prompts. 
Choose one. Focus on telling a story.

title: Main Characters Only: Archetypes, Tropes, and Delusions in Literature and Film
description:
We’re surrounded by stories - on screens, in books, and even in the way we present ourselves online. But what defines a “main character,” and why are certain roles so familiar across time and media?

In this course, you’ll explore how characters are constructed - and how those roles influence the ways we understand identity and narrative in the world around us. We'll look at a range of texts, from Shakespeare and modern novels to TV shows and internet culture, and ask how tropes reflect (and challenge) cultural norms.

Expect open discussions, creative assignments, and the chance to think critically about the roles you see - and maybe the ones you play. No previous experience in English is required - just curiosity and a willingness to engage.

To enroll in this course, please attend the first class meeting to receive a permission code from the student instructor. Questions? Please email student facilitator: Anayaa Jogani anayaajogani@berkeley.edu

title: Issues: Berkeley Medical Journal
description: Each semester in the Berkeley Medical Journal, we showcase original articles by UC Berkeley undergraduates exploring cutting-edge medical innovations, public health issues, and biomedical research in the Bay Area. Students in this DeCal will take on the role of science journalists. Paired with an editor, they will choose their own topics, and write a short report and a feature piece with the chance to be published in the Berkeley Medical Journal. Throughout the semester, students will engage in workshops, peer reviews, and editorial meetings designed to strengthen their scientific communication skills. In addition to guided writing support, students will complete short assignments to deepen their understanding of science journalism, conduct an interview with a research expert, and will have the opportunity to interact with an industry professional at our semesterly guest lecture. The course culminates in a final presentation of each student’s feature article. Selected articles will be considered for publication in the Berkeley Medical Journal.
enrollmentInformation:
There is an application to enroll into this class, however the course is not highly selective and no prior writing experience is expected to join this DeCal. We are looking for students interested in learning about medical journalism, and in helping bridging the gap between academia and the general public.

Our first lecture is on February 3rd, so make sure to apply before then! However, the form will be open during add-drop period for those who decide to join later (until February 11th)

title: Runway and Fashion Design
description: This course is taught in conjunction with the UC Berkeley Fashion Club, Fashion and Student Trends (FAST). The class teaches new designers how to make garments from scratch which will be shown at our semesterly hosted fashion show. Each week designers will learn a new sewing technique to help complete their garments for our show in April.
enrollmentInformation: Fill out design application due January 30th at noon. We will review applications and give out interviews. Everyone admitted into the decal will automatically be members of FAST through the Design Department.

title: Happy Accidents of Biology
description:
Mistakes in science are common, in the lab we’re usually
taught to throw out our experiment and redo it properly. But what about the people
who looked closer at the unusual results they obtained from their “failed”
experiment? And what about the times that paid off?

Each week we will explore how a different accident or mistake led to an
important scientific discovery. We will address the ubiquity of mistakes in science
and the value provided in learning from them, both tangible and intangible.
Students will receive a lecture on the background, mistake made, and outcome.
Afterwards, students will discuss together and with the class the value of the
mistake to the greater scientific community as well as their own view on the
scientific process.

Throughout the course, students will be required to complete:
- two reflections on accidents covered in class
- 1 page research assignment on an accident of their choosing not covered in class
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment is first come, first serve. Please email either facilitator any questions/concerns, we will try our best to accommodate you!

title: Unlocking Research Opportunities
description:
UC Berkeley is one of the world’s leading research universities, filled with opportunities to explore big questions, contribute to cutting-edge discoveries, and learn alongside brilliant researchers. Yet for many undergraduates, getting involved can feel intimidating—shaped by unspoken rules, hidden expectations, and uncertainty about where to even begin. 

This DeCal is designed to make research feel more accessible. Through guided mentorship, practical workshops, and real insight into lab environments, you’ll learn how to identify labs that genuinely excite you, reach out with confidence, and present yourself as a strong research candidate. Beyond joining a lab, you’ll learn how to communicate effectively with mentors, set clear expectations, and develop the habits and mindset that help you grow and thrive as a researcher. 

While this course is especially relevant for students interested in wet-lab research, students from all disciplines are welcome.

title: Video Game Design and Development
description:
**This is a 3 unit course.

This course is a deep dive into the creation of games, from beginning to end. Over the course of the semester, students will pitch a game, form small teams, and build a project from start to finish with help from our facilitators. Beyond developing video games, students will also learn about core game design principles, the roles that exist within the gaming industry today, and how to collaborate with individuals of different skill sets.

The course will be taught using Unity, one of the world’s most popular game engines. Through labs, lectures, and projects, students will master the fundamentals of Unity and will be adequately prepared to work on more complex projects after taking this DeCal.

As a 3-unit course, this class will be rigorous, requiring a lot of time and dedication. Prior game development experience is not required, but it is strongly recommended that a student taking the course has basic art or programming abilities. Students will be selected via application prior to the third week of the semester, to ensure that the class’s composition reflects a diverse range of skills, backgrounds, and proficiency levels.

Our class meets twice a week on Mondays and Thursdays 6:30pm-8pm at Jacobs 10. Attendance is mandatory. If you have a time conflict or cannot attend class, we highly recommend taking this course in a different semester. For more information about the structure of our course, visit our website.

title: Breaking into Case Competition
description:
This course is designed for people who want to build and improve their case competition skills. As a team, we want there to be a mix of skill-building workshops and real-life practice experiences to introduce students to the world of case competitions. The course will cover case competition structure basics, the art of storytelling, financials, topic deep-dives, and building strong presentation skills.

Apply using this form: https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSfjsKtC14_5M9EZxGc8SOKUSBM_fHaYf2Hz9Uip2hoqocHqKw/viewform

title: The Living Epidermis: Skin Microbiome and Dermatology
description:
This course explores the fascinating world of skin biology and the microscopic ecosystem existing on all of our skin surfaces. Students will learn how the skin's layers, resident microbes, and immune defenses work together to maintain health as well as what occurs when that balance is disturbed. 

This course will be split into three main units, mostly discussion heavy. The first unit will focus on understanding the skin and its microbial communities through a series of lectures, discussions, and case studies on conditions like acne, eczema, psoriasis, and fungal infections. Each student will select a skin-related condition or microbe to present on, exploring how disturbances in the skin microbiome may lead to disease or changes in skin health. 

The second unit of the class will be more hands-on through a creative project where students will design and formulate a simple skin-related product with microbiome-friendly/natural ingredients. This is a short unit that introduces the basics of cosmetic formulation, ingredient safety, and the growing field of skin microbiome-safe beauty. 

The final section of the course focuses on how lifestyle, environment, and innovation shape the skin microbiome and dermatology field today. This will be a discussion heavy section where students will read and share thoughts on current research papers and new discoveries in dermatology, cosmeceuticals, and microbiome science. Each student will select a paper or innovation that interests them most and complete a 2-4 page paper. If paper writing is not your forte, any artistic representation of the innovation or related skin condition (ie. creative posters or displays) are also welcome! Just make sure to communicate with the facilitator beforehand.

No prior experience in biology or dermatology is required. Curiosity is the only prerequisite! Grades are based heavily on attendance and participation, with the completion of the three main projects (short presentation, creative product, and short research paper/project). The larger goal of this course is to bring together science, health, and creativity. By the end of the semester, students will have a deeper understanding of how our skin connects biology, environment, and self-care as well as appreciation for the tiny organisms on our skin that we often overlook.
enrollmentInformation: First come, first serve! Fill out the form to receive the permission code! It is NOT meant to be a formal application.

title: K-pop & The Global Music Industry: A&R , Technology, Marketing and Performance
description: This interdisciplinary undergraduate seminar explores the business, artistic, and technological dimensions of K-pop and its global influence. Students will examine artist development, marketing strategies, music technology, and choreography within the K-pop industry. The course integrates hands-on projects, collaborations with university dance groups, case studies, and discussions with industry professionals. Students will create and maintain a blog or a portfolio, to document industry insights throughout the course.
enrollmentInformation: First come first serve. No experience required.

title: Integrative Medicine
description:
Founded in 2004, our DeCal explores integrative and holistic approaches to health through expert-led lectures and interactive discussions. Students engage with leaders in medicine, public health, and research to examine topics such as Food as Medicine, acupuncture, wearable health technology, mind–body practices, and emerging tools in preventive care. Designed to bridge traditional healing systems with modern science, the course encourages critical thinking about how lifestyle, culture, and innovation shape health and well-being.

The course is intentionally low-stress, with minimal assignments and a midterm and final paper. Check out our instagram page to see last year's lectures! https://www.instagram.com/integrativemedicineberkeley/

title: Moot Court
description: This course is offered by UC Berkeley's nationally-ranked undergraduate Moot Court team and is for students interested in appellate advocacy. We'll be using official materials from the American Moot Court Association to help students analyze landmark First Amendment cases, develop legal writing skills and practice oral argumentation.
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served

title: United Nations 101
description:
United Nations 101, offered by the United Nations Association of Berkeley (UNAB), provides an introduction to the history, structure, and global role of the United Nations (UN), crafted in conjunction with the 80th Anniversary of the establishment of the United Nations in 2025. Students will explore key milestones, the evolution of the international order, the UN’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), and current global case studies. The course concludes with a capstone project that challenges students to connect global UN initiatives to local community actions or research projects, where they pursue these topics either through an out-of-class project (i.e. volunteering, advocacy) or a research project (i.e. UN policy analysis).

This course directly supports broader program objectives by developing critical 21st-century competencies including systems thinking, strategic vision, design thinking, problem-solving, anticipatory skills, and interdisciplinary collaboration. The course prepares future professionals with knowledge and skills to address complex, interconnected challenges in their careers and communities. It advances program goals around global citizenship, social responsibility, and preparing graduates to work effectively in diverse national and regional contexts. The SDG framework helps students understand how their field contributes to addressing real-world problems across economic, social, political, and environmental dimensions.

This course is taught by the United Nations Association of Berkeley (UNAB), a chapter of the grassroots non-profit United Nations Association of the United States of America (UNA-USA). Opportunities to get involved with these organizations will be provided.
enrollmentInformation: It’s a first come first serve class. No prerequisites required. The class code is 24423.

title: Introduction to Surgery
description: This class provides practical training in fundamental surgical skills, from knot tying to performing an anastomosis. This course is meant for anyone with an interest in surgery; no prior background knowledge is necessary, and all majors are welcome. We will be starting from basic techniques and working our way up. Along the way, we will discuss several more complicated surgical techniques and procedures, and how they are performed in the operating room. The field of surgery is very intimidating but also intriguing, and we are here to offer you the opportunity to get some hands-on experience.

title: Linux System Administration
description:
This course covers the basics of setting up and administering a production-quality Linux server environment. By the end of this course, we expect that you will…

- be comfortable using GNU/Linux
- understand how different parts of the OS work together, e.g. init, processes, daemons, filesystems, etc.
- understand basic networking on Linux
- have a good sense about maintaining system security
- understand system administration essentials
- get a practical taste of what sysadmins do in industry.

While we expect many students will have a CS background, the only real prerequisite is a desire to learn about new and unfamiliar technologies, which is a critical skill for sysadmins. This course is designed to introduce new users to Linux, as well as give more experienced users a taste of what Linux is capable of!
enrollmentInformation: We will hold infosessions next Tuesday and Thursday. Enrollment (Lab 0) will open after. All information pertaining enrollment will be on the course website.

title: Intro to Real Estate Investing
description:
This course will offer students the opportunity to explore real estate investing through a number of
academic and professional lenses. Students will be introduced to the basic concepts of real property
and the fundamental principles of real estate investments. Sub-industries within real estate
investment will be covered, including banking, private equity, REITs, joint venture development, and
corporate real estate entities.

Readings will include book chapters, industry reports, news articles, and current academic research.
Lectures will review the required readings and ample time will be allotted to discussion.

Presentations by practitioners also will be included. Students will engage with topics through a
combination of group and individual work assignments, quizzes, and case studies that allow the
students to apply what they have learned. The course will feature a final group case-study.
Students will develop the quantitative and qualitative skills necessary to analyze investment projects
and make intelligent decisions about them.
enrollmentInformation: Application System

title: Global Maternal and Child Healh
description:
Join us in discussing the various socioeconomic, cultural, and political factors that affect maternal and child health.
We will examine current efforts in education, public health, and policy to combat disparities in mother and child health. 
Students will also interact with professionals in these fields, all leading to the goal of gaining personal insight.
enrollmentInformation: First come, first served.

title: MUSA 74: Transition to Upper Division Mathematics
description:
The transition from lower division to upper division mathematics courses can be quite daunting even to a very experienced student. Unlike other subjects, the difference between lower and upper division courses in mathematics can be quite overwhelming, the two main culprits being writing proofs and abstract concepts. In this course we will address these issues head-on. In particular, we will learn how to write proofs while developing good mathematical style. We will also give students more familiarity with the mathematical objects appearing in Math 104, Math 110, and Math 113.

MUSA 74 is a 2-unit class which is intended for students would like more familiarity with writing proofs and aren't sure if they're prepared enough for upper-division classes. In particular, we strongly recommend that the class is taken alongside Math 53, 54, or 55. We officially assume no prerequisites other than a little calculus (at the level of Math 51 [formerly Math 1A]), though we will also appeal to Math 53, 54, and 55 for a few examples.

In order to ease the transition, we plan to focus on more of the abstract concepts found in calculus, linear algebra, and differential equations. We will delve into these concepts further by focusing on the proofs that arise when constructing these ideas. By the time you complete this course, you will be comfortable with writing proofs at the level required by the core upper-division sequence of Math 110, Math 113, Math 104, and Math 185.

We want to encourage a welcoming and inclusive learning environment. Questions, curiosity, and collaboration are all highly encouraged, and dismissive attitudes are strongly discouraged. Math is a difficult subject, and confusion is not a sign of weakness. If students would like help outside of class, they are highly encouraged to ask the course facilitators to meet one-on-one.
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment information is first-come first-serve. Details can be found on our discord server.

title: Censorship! Literature and Rhetoric of Modern Book Restrictions
description:
This course will examine contemporary book bans from literary, legal and political lenses and encourage students to engage in critical discussions surrounding educational censorship and the importance of diverse literature. The course will begin by providing the necessary context to have critical discussions on book bans looking at book banning throughout history, examining current political conversations surrounding book banning and aiming to understand relevant legal precedent. The rest of the course will approach banned books from various literary angles, including nonfiction, fiction, children’s literature, and graphic novels. Each work should provide nuance to the issue of book bans and spark engaging and meaningful class discussions.  

Learning in this course will primarily take place through class discussions but will be complemented by occasional brief informational lectures. There will be weekly discussion posts and 1-2 short essays.
enrollmentInformation: Enrollment first come, first served based on the google form linked below! Classes will begin on Monday, February 2

title: Visual Narratives and Storytelling
description:
This DeCal is dedicated to examining various visual narratives in order to teach ourselves, as creators, how to construct our own fictional stories. We will be looking at films, animation, graphic novels, video games, and other miscellaneous fun things, in addition to creating new things for ourselves! 

Our course involves three main focuses: looking, analyzing, and creating. This means 1) critically looking at various visual narratives through different theoretical lenses and utilizing what we see in order to 2) create a visual/verbal narrative project of our own. 

Our texts are meant to be jumping off points from which we can explore our own creative practices and modes of storytelling. As a result of the course, students will be able to look critically at media representations and meanings in multiple cultural and social contexts, observe technical aspects of both the visual and textual, and apply their learning to produce a final creative project.
enrollmentInformation: In order to enroll, interested students must fill out the online application form and attend the first day of class. Enrollment codes are then distributed soon after the first day to students who have fulfilled both requirements.

title: Digital Painting
description:
This course is an introduction to digital art for entertainment, covering painting and drawing techniques applicable to the fields of concept art, visual development, and illustration. This class builds from the basics of digital art, beginning with fundamentals like perspective and values, then exploring further topics such as design and editing. The course culminates in a final illustration, which students work on for several weeks while receiving feedback from both their peers and facilitators. 

This class meets once a week, for two hours. The first half of each class will consist of peer group critiques on the previous week’s homework, followed by a lecture covering a new topic and a related activity. Homework and readings will be assigned in class and due the following week. For more information (course policies, application, full syllabus, etc.), please visit our website or send us an email!

No previous experience using digital art software is required--artists of all experience levels are encouraged to apply! Facilitators are able and happy to instruct students with no previous experience on how to get started with their digital art program of choice. Additionally, drawing tablets can be loaned to students if needed.
enrollmentInformation:
Enrollment is application-based. The application consists of several short answer questions, as well as the creation of a new and original art piece based on a given prompt. (Examples of application pieces created by previous students can be found on our website.) Previous experience in digital (or traditional) art will not play a role in application evaluations; we are looking for students who are passionate and interested in art! 

The prompt for the Spring 2026 semester is: "Forbidden Fruit"

title: DECODE: Startup Success
description:
This course is a speaker-driven startup practicum focused on real-world company building. Each week, students hear directly from startup founders, venture capitalists, and senior operators across Silicon Valley, gaining practical insight into founding, fundraising, scaling, and decision-making at early-stage companies. The course is discussion-based and emphasizes learning from real experiences rather than theory.

The course is intentionally low-overhead. Attendance and in-class participation make up almost the entire grade, with minimal work required outside of class.
enrollmentInformation:
Fill out application to receive enrollment code
email harry_ila@berkeley.edu if you have any questions

title: IEEE Micromouse
description:
The UC Berkeley IEEE Student Branch’s Micromouse DeCal is a hands-on course aimed at undergraduates with an interest in robotics. In the class, teams of ~2 students are formed to build and program autonomous, maze-solving cars that follow the standards set in IEEE’s Micromouse competition.

The course assumes no experience and will be based around a series of labs and project milestones that cover a wide range of robotics concepts. The course will expose students to CircuitPython programming, autonomous navigation, sensors, PID, and basic electrical engineering.

This is a prerequisite-free DeCal; we assume no prior knowledge. In this course, you will learn how to solder (both through-hole and SMD) and how to program on a microcontroller using Python. Ultimately, applying these skills to put together an autonomous maze-solving mouse. Each group is provided a PCB and components free of charge. You will spend the first few sessions of the semester assembling your mouse. Afterwards, we will move on to programming the mouse. Programming labs can typically be completed within the two-hour time slot of our sessions. You may take your mouse home to work on it if you find yourself wanting more time.

We will have weekly 2-hour-long sessions. We may hold office hours/workshops depending on how the course is progressing. The first 20-30 minutes or so of the weekly sessions should be a primer on that week’s content, and the lab documentation will expand on the content covered during lecture. Students will be checked off when they complete each lab.

There will be no exams; your grade will be primarily determined by the completion of your mouse as well as attending the majority of our sessions. Please contact any of the instructors using the micromouse email, or you can find our information on the staff page of the website. DeCals are for your enjoyment, and you will get much more out of them if you attend every session. If you have any questions regarding your standing, please reach out to a staff member.

All necessary parts will be provided to students at no cost.

title: Nuclear War?
description:
Since the end of World War II, nuclear weapons have been an inseparable component of international politics and security. From the Cuban Missile Crisis to Putin’s nuclear threats, states have often attempted to leverage nuclear weapons to deter adversaries and achieve their political objectives. This course explores how nuclear weapons have revolutionized international politics, how states leverage nuclear weapons to achieve political ends, and how individuals have theorized about the causes, conduct, effects, and termination of nuclear war. Key questions we will consider include: What is deterrence, and how have different states practiced nuclear deterrence? What does nuclear escalation look like, and to what extent can nuclear escalation be controlled? What is the relationship between deterrence and proliferation? How do leaders use nuclear weapons to generate political and strategic advantage?

We will begin with an examination of the unique properties of nuclear weapons that defined the Cold War, before turning towards the theory behind nuclear deterrence and strategic stability. Next, we’ll look at how different states practice nuclear deterrence, before analyzing the challenges of extended nuclear deterrence and concluding with an examination of arms races, arms control, and the future of nuclear weapons in the international system. We will meet every week on Tuesday.

The class will include guest speakers such as visiting academics, as well as current and former members of the United States Government, to supplement regular lectures and discussions.

This syllabus is a living document and subject to change. Please bookmark this and check regularly for updates.
enrollmentInformation: The first class begins 2/7

title: Research 101: From Question to Publication
description:
This course is designed primarily for underclassmen with limited prior research experience, but students at all levels are welcome! Our goal is to equip students with the tools and confidence to take initiative in pursuing undergraduate research and developing their own projects by the end of the course. We will also help new researchers feel less overwhelmed as they transition into a lab environment. In addition, the course will provide guidance on how to share research outcomes, covering opportunities such as symposiums, peer-reviewed journals, and funding avenues.

You will learn how to think critically after developing a research question. This will provide yo with a foundation to engage in your own research after learning different types of research, methods, the experimental or data collection process, writing, and insight into publication/presentations. Throughout the semester, you will engage in the research process by exploring primary scientific literature, developing research proposals, drafting final papers, and ultimately creating a research poster and elevator-pitch presentation for the end-of-semester symposium.
enrollmentInformation: Just fill out the form below for us to send you the enrollment number! All applications will be accepted until we fill up.

title: Israeli Military History
description:
This course provides a comprehensive study of the military history of Israel, from the pre-state
militias to ongoing conflicts. We will examine key wars and operations, analyze military
strategies and outcomes, and explore the socio-political implications of these conflicts. Through
lectures and discussions, students will gain a deep understanding of the evolution of Israel's
military and its impact on the region and the world.
enrollmentInformation: Fill out the application form and we will reach out to you with the course code!

title: Career Exploration and Planing Decal
description: The objective of this course is to arm you with the tools necessary to embark on a successful career journey. As a college student with many prospects, it can be daunting to know where to begin, so we will guide them through exploring potential career paths, the internship and job search, resume and cover letter writing, networking, interview skills, and highlighting your strengths as an applicant.
enrollmentInformation: We look forward to reading your application to join this decal for the spring semester of 2026. We base our decisions on your written responses and will read applications as they are submitted but we do not make our decisions on a first-come first-serve basis. We will reach out to you regarding your status after the application deadline for further details.

title: Learn to Play the Sather Tower Bells — A Carillon Study
description:
This DeCal aims to teach interested students the basic history and mechanics of the carillon and the carillon community. Students will also learn basic carillon playing technique through private student mentoring, and perform a piece of their own choosing or from standard carillon repertoire.

Students should know how a keyboard functions and be able to read duple and triple time signatures through three sharps and flats. Piano experience is not required; we welcome all musical backgrounds.
enrollmentInformation: Due to the popularity of this course, spots are extremely limited. Preference will be given to students in their final year who have applied for the DeCal in the past. Application link:

title: Tamarkoz: Sufi Meditation
description:
This course focuses on learning Tamarkoz (Sufi Meditation), which is a heart-centered meditation practice, through in-class guided instruction. In each class, you will be warmly guided through deep breathing exercises, mind and body relaxation techniques, gentle stretches, slow movement meditation, visualization/imagery, and heart-focused meditation. In Sufism, the heart is considered to be our stable center, and the gateway to our spiritual realm, through which we can access our innate and endowed knowledge and wisdom.

Tamarkoz is a profound stress management technique; a holistic and practical way of recovering and maintaining health, balance and discipline in life; and an opportunity to explore the mysteries of the heart and discover the treasures within yourself. Anyone can practice and benefit from Tamarkoz regardless of background, beliefs and/or physical ability. No prior experience with meditation is necessary. All are warmly welcome.

To pass this class, students must attend class each week, practice the Sufi meditation techniques and exercises they learn in class for one hour each week outside of class (does not need to be in one sitting), complete the assigned reading each week, and submit written reading reflections and practice reflections in bCourses each week. A final paper is required to pass
enrollmentInformation:
This DeCal enrolls students on a first-come first-served basis, based on the timestamp of your application: https://bit.ly/TamarkozSpring2026Reg. Class begins on Friday, February 6. Feel free to email us with any questions at sufimeditation.decal@berkeley.edu.

We respectfully ask that you do not request a spot in the class unless you are sincerely interested in learning Sufi Meditation and are 100% committed to attending class each week and completing your weekly assignments. Serious applicants only, please. 

Feel free to email us with any questions at sufimeditation.decal@berkeley.edu.

title: Photography Principles
description: In this class, students will be introduced to the history, art, and practices of photography in the modern world. Students will learn how to manually operate a digital SLR camera, how to use lighting in conjunction with posing to create compositions, and how to post-process RAW photos. Different disciplines will also be taught and include portraiture, landscape, architecture, product, studio, and more. Over the semester, students will learn critiques of photos and develop a more artistic eye for photos.
enrollmentInformation: Application. A google form will open on our home website, innovativedesign.club, as well as being listed on the DeCal website.

title: Introduction to Photoshop and Illustrator
description: This course teaches graphic design through the use of Photoshop and Illustrator. Adobe Photoshop and Illustrator CC will be taught in a series of tutorials complemented with exercises to perform during class and at home. These exercises give students the opportunity to practice the tools, create designs, and exercise their creativity. We will also explore graphic design trends and their applications, and learn to receive and give critiques in order to improve your design work.
enrollmentInformation: Application. A google form will open on our home website, innovativedesign.club, as well as being listed on the DeCal website.

title: Pre-Law DeCal
description: Hi Cal students, interested in the legal field? This 1-unit course provides students interested in the legal field with the foundations of the LSAT, law school applications, and the law school experience. We will cover these topics through lectures and peer discussions. If you are unsure if you are 100% pre-law, that is okay! Students who take this course will reflect personally on why they do or do not want to attend law school. Throughout the DeCal, we will focus on different career paths in the legal field and on legal internship opportunities at Cal and beyond. Additionally, we hope to provide you with the resources, support, and guidance to better understand law school. We will have guest speakers from Berkeley Law and the USF School of Law. Our mission is to provide all pre-law students with the opportunity to gain greater exposure, confidence, and knowledge of the legal field.
enrollmentInformation: First-come, first-served basis until all seats are filled. You can enroll in the DeCal through CalCentral using our course code #23410. If you have any issues enrolling in the course, please feel free to email me at suzettec@berkeley.edu. Happy start of Spring 2026!

title: Cal Pokémon Academy
description: Cal Pokémon Academy celebrates and explores all things Pokémon! CPA covers the complete history of the Pokémon franchise, from popular media to meta-topics like marketing strategy and the competitive circuit. We also discuss social issues within the Pokéverse, then delve into the theoretical science behind your favorite creatures. Whether you’re a naïve Youngster or a seasoned Veteran, CPA will help you catch up on over two decades of developments, gain a new appreciation for the franchise, and make new friends on your journey to be the very best (like no one ever was).

title: Runway and Fashion Design
description:
This course is taught in conjunction with the UC Berkeley Fashion Club, Fashion and Student Trends (FAST).
The class teaches new designers how to make garments from scratch which will be shown at our semesterly hosted fashion show. Each week designers will learn a new sewing technique to help complete their garments for our fashion show in April.
enrollmentInformation:
Enrollment Process: fill out the design application due January 30th at noon. We will review applications and give out interviews. 
Everyone admitted into the decal will automatically be members of FAST through the Design Department.

title: Introduction to Manual Dexterity in Healthcare
description: Manual Dexterity in Healthcare is an interactive, hands-on class where students discover how important steady hands and good coordination are in healthcare careers. Through skill building activities, students will practice precision, control, and hand eye coordination, the same skills used by dentists, surgeons, and other healthcare professionals.

title: Climate Change in Media
description: This course focuses on how climate change is framed to the public. As the threat of climate change increases daily, we must analyze how climate change is framed, because the media can convince people of almost anything. This class is going to analyze how climate change is portrayed through various forms of media, including writing, documentaries, news outlets, and our daily surroundings. We will particularly be focusing on the concept of environmental justice when examining all these sources. In order to properly address climate change, we must build a framework that not only protects our planet but also ensures that the burdens and benefits of a sustainable future are shared equitably among all communities, leaving no one behind.
enrollmentInformation: First Come First Served
